1.1: polumeros kai polytropōs — "at many times and in many ways" (polumeros: in many portions/parts; polytropōs: in many modes/manners): the adverbial pair describes the OT revelation's characteristic pluralism — different authors, genres, periods, and literary forms. The contrast is with the Son's single, definitive, eschatological speaking. Palaios (long ago) points to the temporal distance of the OT revelation; ep' eschatou tōn hēmerōn toutōn (at the end of these days) is the eschatological marker that the Son's speech is the final word in the series.
1.2: hon ethēken klēronomon pantōn — "whom he appointed heir of all things" (klēronomon: inheritor, heir): the appointment (ethēken, placed/set) precedes the creation in salvation-historical logic — the heir is appointed before the estate is created. Di' hou kai epoiēsen tous aiōnas — "through whom he also made the ages" (aiōnas: ages, worlds — not merely the temporal ages but the entire created order in its temporal unfolding). The Son as both heir and creator establishes his double relationship to the created order: he made it and he will receive it.
1.3:
apaugasma tēs doxēs kai charakter tēs hypostaseōs autou — "the radiance of his glory and the exact imprint of his nature" (apaugasma: radiance, effulgence — that which streams out from the source of light; charakter: the impression made by an engraving tool, the exact stamp/character — NT hapax): the two metaphors are complementary. Apaugasma describes the continuous streaming-out of glory from its source (like light from the sun — the sun's radiance is not the sun itself but is genuinely from it and conveys it). Charakter describes the exact correspondence between the impression and the seal — the Son bears the exact impression of the Father's hypostasis (substance, essential nature). Both terms are drawn from the Hellenistic philosophical and wisdom traditions.

pheron ta panta tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou — "upholding all things by the word of his power" (pheron: bearing, carrying, upholding — present active participle: continuously upholding): the Son's sustaining role in creation is present-tense and ongoing. Tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou (by the word of his power) — not by effort but by the same creative word that made all things.
1.4: tosoutō kreittōn genomenos tōn angelōn — "having become so much better than the angels" (kreittōn: superior, better — a key term in Hebrews used 13 times; genomenos: having become — aorist participle marking the historical event of the Son's exaltation): the Son's superiority is not merely ontological but the result of the exaltation after humiliation. The "becoming better" does not imply a prior equality with angels but the eschatological manifestation of what was always true. Diaphorōteron par' autous keklēronomēken onoma — "he has inherited a more excellent name than they" (keklēronomēken: perfect active — permanently inherited): the perfect tense indicates a permanent acquisition. The "name" is the Son-name (v5).
1.5: Hyios mou ei sy, egō sēmeron gegennēka se — "You are my Son; today I have begotten you" (Ps 2:7 LXX): the sēmeron (today) is the eschatological "today" of the resurrection-exaltation (Acts 13:33 applies this text to the resurrection). The divine begetting is not eternal generation (though that may be implied in the background) but the eschatological declaration of the Son's identity at the exaltation event. Egō esomai autō eis patera kai autos estai moi eis huion (2 Sam 7:14 LXX) — the Davidic covenant promise extended beyond its original Solomonic application to the definitive Son.
1.7: ho poiōn tous angelous autou pneumata kai tous leitourgous autou pyros phloga — "he makes his messengers winds, and his servants a flame of fire" (Ps 104:4 LXX): leitourgous (liturgical servants, ministers — the same word as the priestly and Levitical ministers): angels are characterized as servants/liturgists whose mode of being is as changeable as wind and fire — they serve but they do not reign. The contrast with the eternal Son (vv8-12) is the rhetorical point.
1.8: pros de ton huion, Ho thronos sou, ho theos, eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos — "But of the Son he says: Your throne, O God, is forever and ever" (Ps 45:6 LXX): the vocative ho theos addressed to the Son is one of the strongest direct divine-title applications in the NT. The Psalm 45 text, originally a royal wedding song, addresses the Davidic king as elohim (God/divine one) — Hebrews reads this as a divine address to the Son that confirms his divine status. Eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos (unto the age of the age = forever): the eternal duration of the Son's throne contrasts with the angels' servant role.
1.10: Sy kat' archas, Kyrie, tēn gēn ethemeliōsas, kai erga tōn cheirōn sou eisin hoi ouranoi — "You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands" (Ps 102:25-27 LXX): the citation addresses Kyrios (LORD = YHWH in the OT) but Hebrews applies it to the Son — a Christological reading of a Yahweh-text. The permanent stability of the creator (sy de diameneis = you remain) contrasted with the perishing creation establishes the Son's divine eternity over against the angels who, like the creation, are changeable.
1.13: pros tina de tōn angelōn eirēken pote, Kathou ek dexiōn mou heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou — "But to which of the angels has he ever said: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet?" (Ps 110:1 LXX): the rhetorical question clinches the argument — God has never addressed any angel with the right-hand session command. Heōs an (until) — the session is not permanent stasis but active waiting for the eschatological subjugation. The verse inaugurates the rest-and-waiting theme that will recur throughout Hebrews.
1.14: ouchi pantes eisin leitourgika pneumata eis diakonian apostellomena dia tous mellontas klēronomein sōtērian — "Are they not all ministering spirits sent out to serve for the sake of those who are to inherit salvation?" (leitourgika pneumata: liturgical/ministerial spirits — leitourgikos is the adjective of priestly/liturgical service; the angels as liturgical servants stands in contrast to the Son as liturgical high priest). The rhetorical question expects a "yes": all angels are servants-for-others, not seated lords.
1.6:
hotan de palin eisagagē ton prōtotokon eis tēn oikoumenēn — "And again, when he brings the firstborn into the world" (palin: again — the particle is ambiguous; it may connect to "he says" [another citation follows] or modify eisagagē [bringing in a second time, i.e., the second coming]; most likely it serves as a connective introducing the next citation): prōtotokon (firstborn — G4416: prōtotokos) carries the double weight of priority and privilege — in Jewish inheritance law the firstborn holds the highest claim. The "bringing into the world" (eis tēn oikoumenēn) refers either to the incarnation or the eschatological enthronement.

proskynesatōsan autō pantes angeloi theou — "Let all God's angels worship him" (from Deut 32:43 LXX or Ps 97:7): proskynesatōsan (third-person aorist imperative of proskyneō: to worship, to bow before) — the divine command that all angels bow to the Son makes explicit what the entire catena of citations argues: the Son receives divine honor that no angel can claim or receive.
1.9:
ēgapēsas dikaiosynēn kai emisēsas anomian — "You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness" (Ps 45:7): the perfect tenses (ēgapēsas, emisēsas) describe a completed moral orientation that defines the king's character — not a momentary act but an established disposition of the will. The polarity dikaiosynē/anomia (righteousness/lawlessness) is absolute; the king has not merely preferred righteousness but actively hated its opposite.

echrien se ho theos, ho theos sou, elaion agalliaseōs para tous metochous sou — "therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions" (echrien: anointed — aorist of chriō [G5548], the verb behind christos): the citation activates the messianic register of the Psalm — the one addressed as ho theos (v8) is also the one anointed by God (ho theos sou). The vocative + nominative address in vv8-9 distinguishes the addressee (the Son) and the anointer (the Father). Elaion agalliaseōs (oil of gladness) — the anointing oil is not cultic but celebratory, marking exaltation over all companions (metochous: partners/sharers — later used for Christ's "partners" in Heb 3:14).
1.11: autoi apolountai, sy de diameneis — "They will perish, but you remain" (Ps 102:26 LXX): the contrast is ontological and grammatical: apolountai (they shall perish — future middle: their destruction is their own end) vs. diameneis (you remain through-and-through — dia intensifies the permanence). The heavens and earth are the antecedent of "they" — the entire created cosmos is subject to decay, while the Son endures. Pantes hōs himation palaióthēsontai (all of them shall grow old like a garment) — the aging metaphor: garments that once served their function become thread-worn and discardable.
1.12: hōsei peribolaion helixeis autous kai allagēsontai, sy de ho autos ei kai ta etē sou ouk ekleipsousin — "You will roll them up like a robe; like a garment they will be changed. But you are the same, and your years will never end" (Ps 102:27 LXX): peribolaion (robe, wrapper — G4018: something wrapped around): the Creator rolls up the cosmos as a garment rolled up after use — the image speaks to the deliberate sovereign act of the Son at the end of the age. Allagēsontai (they shall be changed — future passive): the heavens and earth are subject to change, not the Son. Sy de ho autos ei — "but you are the same" (ho autos: the same, unchanged — applied to the Son as the citation's referent): Hebrews reads the Ps 102 Kyrios-address as addressed to the Son, identifying the Son's divine immutability with the LORD of the Psalm. Ta etē sou ouk ekleipsousin (your years will not fail/fall short): ekleipsousin from ekleipō — to fail, to be eclipsed (as in a solar eclipse); the Son's years cannot be eclipsed by any temporal end.

2.1: dei perissoteros prosechein hēmas tois akoustheisin, mēpote pararrūomen — "we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it" (pararrūomen: to drift past, to flow past — a nautical term for a ship drifting past its harbor because the pilot failed to steer properly): the image of passive drifting is the warning against neglect rather than active apostasy. The comparative perissoteros (more exceedingly) grounds the increased attentiveness requirement in the increased magnitude of the new covenant revelation.
2.3: pōs hēmeis ekpheuxometha tēlikaytēs amelēsantes sōtērias — "how shall we escape if we neglect such a great salvation?" (amelēsantes: having neglected, having cared nothing for — aorist participle): amelēo (to be unconcerned about, to neglect) implies passive indifference rather than active rejection — the warning is against those who simply do not take salvation seriously. The tēlikautēs (so great) grounds the impossibility of escape: the magnitude of the salvation corresponds to the magnitude of the consequence for neglecting it.
2.9: ton de brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon blepomen Iēsoun dia to pathēma tou thanatou doxē kai timē estephanōmenon — "but we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death" (brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon: made lower for a little — the Ps 8 phrase applied to the incarnate humiliation): the name Iēsoun (Jesus) appears here for the first time in Hebrews, positioned after the description of humiliation — the human name of the one who took the human condition. Doxē kai timē estephanōmenon (crowned with glory and honor) — the perfect passive participle indicates the permanent crown of the exaltation state.
2.10: eprepen gar autō... ton archēgon tēs sōtērias autōn dia pathēmatōn teleiōsai — "it was fitting for him... to make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering" (archēgon: pioneer, captain, founder — one who leads the way, the first to go through a path that others will follow; teleiōsai: to perfect, complete, bring to the goal — the teleio- root pervades Hebrews): archēgos is not merely an author who initiates from a distance but a pioneer who personally traverses the route. The perfection through suffering is not moral improvement (the Son was not morally deficient) but the completion of the vocational path — the suffering was necessary for the high-priestly qualification.
2.11: ho te gar hagiazōn kai hoi hagiazomenoi ex henos pantes — "for he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source" (ex henos pantes: all from one — the henos is either masculine [one father, i.e., Abraham, or God] or neuter [one origin/source]): the shared origin establishes the solidarity that makes the Son's brotherhood genuine rather than merely analogical. Di' hēn aitian ouk epaischynetai adelphous autous kalein — "for which reason he is not ashamed to call them brothers" (ouk epaischynetai: is not ashamed — the social-shame context: the exalted one acknowledging his solidarity with the lowly).
2.17: hothen ōpheilen kata panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai, hina eleēmōn genētai kai pistos archiereus ta pros ton theon eis to hilaskesthai tas hamartias tou laou — "therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest in the service of God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people" (hilaskesthai: to propitiate, make atonement — the LXX verb for the priestly atonement act on the Day of Atonement [Lev 16:20 LXX]; used for covering/averting divine wrath against sin): the hilaskesthai debate centers on whether "expiation" (neutralizing sin's pollution) or "propitiation" (averting God's wrath) is primary. The LXX usage and the wrath-context of Hebrews favor propitiation, though the two are not sharply separable. Eleēmōn kai pistos (merciful and faithful) — the two qualifications for a legitimate high priest who genuinely represents the people before God.
2.18: en hō gar peponthen autos peirastheis, dynatai tois peirazomenois boēthēsai — "for because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted" (peponthen: perfect passive of paschō = to suffer; peirastheis: having been tested/tempted — aorist passive participle): the perfect tense of peponthen (he has suffered, with the suffering-effect remaining) grounds the present capacity to help. The suffering is not merely past but bears on his present qualification. Boēthēsai (to come to the aid, to help — from boaō = to shout + theō = to run): to run to the cry of one who calls for help — the helping is urgent and responsive.
2.2:
ei gar ho di' angelōn lalētheis logos egeneto bebaios — "For if the message declared through angels was reliable" (bebaios: firm, reliable, legally valid — a legal-commercial term for a guarantee that stands; used in papyri for a deed that conveys secure ownership): the Torah was understood in Second Temple tradition to have been mediated by angels (Deut 33:2 LXX; Gal 3:19; Acts 7:53) — its reliability (bebaios) is the argumentative baseline. If angel-mediated law carried penal force, how much more does Son-mediated salvation demand a response.

kai pasa parabasis kai parakoē elaben endikon misthapodosian — "and every transgression and act of disobedience received a just penalty" (parabasis: transgression, stepping over a boundary [para + bainō]; parakoē: disobedience, failure to hear fully [para + akouō]): the two terms cover active violation of explicit command (parabasis) and passive failure to heed (parakoē). Endikos misthapodosian (just reward/penalty — misthapodosia: payment of wages due): the penalty is not arbitrary but proportional and legally due.
2.4:
synepimartyroutos theou sēmeiois te kai terasin kai poikilais dynamesin kai pneumatos hagiou merismoiis kata tēn autou thelēsin — "while God confirmed their message with signs, wonders, various miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will" (synepimartyroutos: co-testifying with, bearing joint-witness — syn + epi + martyreō: an intensified compound; God's confirmatory witness accompanied the apostolic message): the three nouns sēmeia (signs), terata (wonders), dynameis (acts of power, miracles) are a stock NT formulaic sequence for the miraculous attestation of divine activity.

pneumatos hagiou merismoiis kata tēn autou thelēsin — "gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will" (merismoiis: distributions, dividings-out — from merizō; merismos implies sovereign apportionment): the Spirit-gifts are not human attainments but divine distributions allocated according to God's own will (kata tēn autou thelēsin), not the recipient's worthiness or request.
2.5: ou gar angelois hypetaxen tēn oikoumenēn tēn mellousan, peri hēs laloumen — "For God did not subject the coming world — the world we are speaking about — to angels" (oikoumenēn tēn mellousan: the coming inhabited world — oikoumenē is the inhabited earth/Roman world, but here in its eschatological sense, the world-to-come): the parenthetical peri hēs laloumen (about which we speak) identifies the topic: the coming world is the realm under discussion throughout. Angels govern the present age (understood in Second Temple cosmology); the coming age will not be theirs.
2.6: diemartyrato de pou tis legōn, Ti estin anthrōpos hoti mimnēskē autou, ē huios anthrōpou hoti episkeptē auton — "But there is a place where someone has testified: What is man that you are mindful of him, or the son of man that you care for him?" (Ps 8:4 LXX): the indefinite attribution (diemartyrato de pou tis: testified somewhere someone) is a deliberate distancing device — Hebrews cites the Psalm's divine authority, not the human author's identity. Ti estin anthrōpos (What is man?) — the Psalm's original frame is human smallness contrasted with God's grandeur. Hebrews will read it as a question whose only adequate answer is the Son of Man who became low and was exalted. Episkeptē (you care for/visit — from episkeptomai: to look upon with care, to visit with saving intent; the word carries the covenantal visit-to-rescue connotation).
2.7: ēlatōsas auton brachy ti par' angelous, doxē kai timē estephanōsas auton, kai katestēsas auton epi ta erga tōn cheirōn sou — "You made him a little lower than the angels; you crowned him with glory and honor and set him over the works of your hands" (Ps 8:5-6 LXX): brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōsas (you made lower by a little than/beside angels — ēlatōsas: aorist of elattoō, to diminish, lower; brachy ti: a little, for a short time): Hebrews will read the "little" as both degree (slightly lower in rank) and duration (for a brief time of humiliation). The LXX reads the Hebrew elohim as "angels" — Hebrews follows the LXX. Estephanōsas (you crowned — aorist): the crowning is the exaltation-event, historically completed.
2.8: panta hypetaxas hypokatō tōn podōn autou... nyn de oupō horōmen autō ta panta hypotetagmena — "You have put everything under his feet... yet at present we do not see everything subject to him": panta hypetaxas hypokatō tōn podōn autou (Ps 8:6b LXX) — "everything" is the universal scope, including the angels. En tō gar hypotaxai autō ta panta, ouden aphēken autō anypotakton (in the subjecting of all things to him, he left nothing not subjected to him) — the grammar is deliberate: the aorist subjection (hypotaxai) is comprehensive in scope but the present experience does not yet match it. Nyn de oupō horōmen (but now not yet we see) — the "not yet" is eschatological, not an admission of failure; the tension between "all things subjected" and "not yet visible" is resolved in v9 by pointing to the one who already went through death and was crowned.
2.12: legōn, Apaggeleō to onoma sou tois adelphois mou, en mesō ekklēsias hymnēsō se — "saying: I will proclaim your name to my brothers and sisters; in the midst of the assembly I will sing your praise" (Ps 22:22 LXX): the citation puts Ps 22 in the mouth of the Son — the Psalm that begins with the cry of dereliction (Ps 22:1, quoted at the cross) ends with the resolution to proclaim God's name to the community. Apaggeleō (I will report/announce — a strong public proclamation); en mesō ekklēsias (in the midst of the assembly/church — ekklēsia): the word the NT uses for the gathered community appears here in an OT psalm, read as the Son's own declaration of solidarity and praise.
2.13:
kai palin, Egō esomai pepoithōs ep' autō — "And again, I will trust in him" (Isa 8:17 LXX): a second OT citation placed in the Son's mouth. Pepoithōs ep' autō (trusting upon him — perfect participle of peithō: to convince; perfect = standing in a state of settled trust): the Son himself models the faith-posture to which Hebrews calls its community — trust in God even through suffering and apparent divine hiddenness, which is exactly the Isaiah 8 context (God hiding his face while the remnant trusts).

kai palin, Idou egō kai ta paidia ha moi edōken ho theos — "and again, Here am I and the children God has given me" (Isa 8:18 LXX): paidia (children — G3813, a term of smallness and dependence): the Son presents "the children" as those entrusted to him — the same solidary language as v11 (adelphous) and v14 (ta paidia). The double Isa 8 citation establishes the Son as the Servant-remnant figure who trusts God and maintains solidarity with his people through the dark time.
2.14:
epei oun ta paidia kekoinōnēken haimatos kai sarkos kai autos paraplēsiōs metesche tōn autōn — "Since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise took part in these" (kekoinōnēken: perfect active — they have and continue to share in flesh and blood, an established condition; metesche: aorist — he took part, a historical once-for-all act): the contrast of tenses is precise — the children's flesh-and-blood existence is their ongoing state; the Son's taking part is the historical incarnation event. Paraplēsiōs (in like manner, closely parallel — a comparative adverb expressing real resemblance, not mere analogy).

hina dia tou thanatou katargēsē ton to kratos echonta tou thanatou, tout' estin ton diabolon — "so that through death he might destroy the one who holds the power of death — that is, the devil" (katargēsē: aorist subjunctive of katargeō: to render inoperative, to nullify, to bring to nothing — not annihilation but de-powering): the means of destruction is the weapon itself — death is used against the death-holder. Ton to kratos echonta tou thanatou (the one having the power/strength of death): kratos (G2904) is brute power/dominion — the devil's grip on death is expressed as a possession he holds, not an inherent attribute.
2.15: kai apallaxē toutous, hosoi phobō thanatou dia pantos tou zēn enochoi ēsan douleias — "and free all those who all their lives had been held in slavery by their fear of death" (apallaxē: aorist subjunctive of apallassō: to release, set free, rid of — medical use for relieving a patient of disease): phobō thanatou (fear of death) — phobos here is the enslaving dread that structures human life under the threat of mortality. Enochoi ēsan douleias (were held liable to/subject to bondage): enochos (G1777) is a legal term — one who is bound/guilty; the slavery is legally constituted by the liability that the fear of death imposes on human existence.
2.16: ou gar dēpou angelōn epilambanetai, alla spermatos Abraam epilambanetai — "For surely it is not angels he helps, but Abraham's descendants" (epilambanetai: to take hold of, to seize, to grasp — present middle; the verb is vivid: the Son decisively lays hold of Abraham's seed): ou gar dēpou angelōn (for surely not of angels — dēpou is an emphatic particle asserting what is obvious or certain): the Son's assumption of human nature was directed specifically at Abraham's seed. Spermatos Abraam (Abraham's seed — sperma): the corporate seed of Abraham, the covenant people, is the specific humanity the Son joined — not human nature in general, and not angels at all.

3.1: katanoeisate ton apostolon kai archierea tēs homologias hēmōn Iēsoun — "consider Jesus, the apostle and high priest of our confession" (katanoeisate: aorist imperative of katanoeō, to observe carefully, to fix one's attention on — the kata intensifies the observation): Jesus is called apostolos (apostle, sent one) only here in the NT — the term that the community uses for its own commissioned messengers is applied to the Son as the original sent-one from the Father. Homologias (confession): the common confession of faith that the community shares and that defines them.
3.6: Christos de hōs huios epi ton oikon autou — "but Christ is faithful over God's house as a Son" (epi ton oikon: over the house — the preposition epi with accusative indicates authority over): the Son's position is qualitatively different from Moses's en (within) the house — the Son is over it as the builder and heir. Hou oikos esmen hēmeis (whose house we are) — the community is identified as God's household; the ecclesiology is household-shaped. Eanper tēn parrēsian kai to kauchēma tēs elpidos mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "if indeed we hold fast our confidence and our boasting in our hope firm to the end."
3.13: alla parakaleite heautous kath' hekastēn hēmeran, achris hou to Sēmeron kaleitai, hina mē sklērunthē tis ex hymōn apatē tēs hamartias — "but exhort one another every day, as long as it is called today, that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" (apatē tēs hamartias: the deceitfulness of sin — apatē is deceit, delusion; sin is personified as a deceiver who leads the mind astray from reality): sklērunthē (be hardened — aorist passive subjunctive of sklērunō): hardening is a process that daily mutual exhortation resists. The "today" of Ps 95 is the present moment of the community's hearing — a kairos that closes when the heart hardens.
3.14: metochoi gar tou Christou gegonamen, eanper tēn archēn tēs hypostaseōs mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "for we have come to share in Christ, if indeed we hold our original confidence firm to the end" (metochoi tou Christou: partakers/sharers of Christ — metochos appears 6x in Hebrews; sharing/participation is a key Hebrews concept): hypostaseōs — the same word as in 1:3 (charakter tēs hypostaseōs); here it means the foundational confidence/assurance. The sharing in Christ is conditioned on maintaining the beginning (archēn) of that assurance to the end — the condition is not soteriological uncertainty but the community's perseverance in the faith-life.
3.2: piston onti tō poiēsanti auton, hōs kai Mōysēs en holō tō oikō autou — "He was faithful to the one who appointed him, just as Moses was faithful in all God's house" (pistos: faithful, trustworthy — G4103): the comparison with Moses is the tertium comparationis — faithfulness. The ground-point is the same (faithfulness to the one who appointed); the contrast will be the position (v5-6: servant vs. son). Tō poiēsanti auton (to the one who made/appointed him — poieō here carries the sense of appointment/commissioning, not creation): the Son's faithfulness is to his vocational appointment, not a statement about origins.
3.3: pleionos gar doxēs houtos para Mōysēn ēxiōtai, kath' hoson pleiona timēn echei tou oikou ho kataskeuasas auton — "But Jesus has been found worthy of greater honor than Moses — just as the builder of a house deserves more honor than the house itself" (ēxiōtai: perfect passive of axioō: to account worthy — permanently counted as worthy): the comparison is structural: builder > house in honor. Moses is part of the house; the Son built it. Pleionos doxēs (more glory — genitive of comparison with pleionos): the comparison is about degree of glory, not denial of Moses's glory; the greater/lesser structure is the rhetorical engine of Hebrews throughout.
3.4: pas gar oikos kataskeuazetai hypo tinos, ho de panta kataskeuasas theos — "Every house is built by someone, but God is the builder of everything" (kataskeuazetai hypo tinos: is constructed by someone — passive, with a builder implied): the premise is universal (every house requires a builder) and leads to the inference that if the Son built the house (v3), he participates in the divine building role, since all building ultimately traces back to God. The verse grounds the Son's superiority to Moses in the Son's participation in divine creative agency.
3.5: kai Mōysēs men pistos en holō tō oikō autou hōs therapōn, eis martyrion tōn lalēthēsomenōn — "Moses was faithful as a servant in all God's house, giving testimony to what would be said in the future" (therapōn: servant, attendant — G2324, a word of dignity; not the ordinary doulos [slave] but a personal attendant in a position of honor; used of Moses only in the LXX [Num 12:7]): the servant-position is honorable but subordinate. Eis martyrion tōn lalēthēsomenōn (for a testimony of the things that would be spoken): Moses's entire ministry was anticipatory — a forward-pointing witness to the realities that would be spoken in the Son.
3.7: dio kathōs legei to pneuma to hagion, Sēmeron ean tēs phōnēs autou akousēte — "So, as the Holy Spirit says: Today, if you hear his voice," (Ps 95:7-8 LXX): the attribution to to pneuma to hagion (the Holy Spirit) as the one who speaks (legei — present tense) makes the Psalm's address present and ongoing: the Spirit continues to speak this word to the community. Sēmeron (today) — the Psalm's urgent temporal marker that Hebrews will develop into the "today" of the community's hearing moment.
3.8: mē sklērynēte tas kardias hymōn hōs en tō parapikrasmō, kata tēn hēmeran tou peirasmou en tē erēmō — "do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion, on the day of testing in the wilderness" (sklērynēte: aorist subjunctive of sklērunō: to harden, to make stiff/callous — the aorist expresses a completed act of hardening): parapikrasmos (rebellion, embitterment — G3894, from pikraino: to make bitter; the prefix para adds directional force — a bitter opposition against God): the LXX translates the place-names Meribah and Massah as events (rebellion/testing) rather than preserving the Hebrew toponyms. The warning is against the internal act of hardening — a volitional stiffening against the voice.
3.9: hou epeirasan hoi pateres hymōn en dokimasia kai eidon ta erga mou tessarakonta etē — "where your ancestors tested and tried me, though they saw my works for forty years" (epeirasan: tested — from peirazō: to test, to put to proof; dokimasia: testing/proving — here in apposition, intensifying): the paradox is striking: forty years of seeing the works and yet testing the worker. The sustained duration of the wilderness miracles makes the continued testing inexcusable. The "testing" of God is the inverse of proper faith: treating God as unproven rather than trusting the demonstrated record.
3.10: dio prosōchthisa tē genea tautē kai eipon, Aei planōntai tē kardia, autoi de ouk egnōsan tas hodous mou — "That is why I was angry with that generation; I said, Their hearts are always going astray, and they have not known my ways" (prosōchthisa: I was disgusted/wearied with — from prosochtizō: a strong word for angry disgust, being fed up; the divine emotional response to sustained rebellion): aei planōntai (always they go astray — present tense: a continuous wandering): the heart-wandering is habitual, not occasional. Ouk egnōsan tas hodous mou (they did not know my ways): egnōsan — the knowing is relational/experiential (not merely intellectual) knowledge of how God works; they experienced the ways but refused to recognize them.
3.11: hōs ōmosa en tē orgē mou, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou — "So I swore in my anger, They shall never enter my rest" (Ps 95:11 LXX): ōmosa en tē orgē mou (I swore in my anger): the divine oath is made at the height of provocation, making it immovable. The Greek construction Ei eiseleusontai translates a Hebrew oath formula (ʾim + imperfect) that means "they shall absolutely not enter" — the "if" is not conditional but a strong negative assertion carried by the oath. Katapausin mou (my rest) — the rest is God's own rest, the eschatological Sabbath rest that the wilderness generation forfeited.
3.12: blepete, adelphoi, mēpote estai en tini hymōn kardia ponēra apistias en tō apostēnai apo theou zōntos — "See to it, brothers and sisters, that none of you has a sinful, unbelieving heart that turns away from the living God" (blepete: watch/see carefully — present imperative, ongoing vigilance required): kardia ponēra apistias (an evil heart of unbelief — ponēra: wicked, harmful; apistias: genitive of apistia, unbelief): the evil heart is not characterized by gross sin but by the specific evil of apistia — failure to trust. Apostēnai apo theou zōntos (to fall away from the living God — apistanai: to stand away from, to defect; the word behind "apostasy"): the departure is directional — away from the living God. Theou zōntos (living God) — the life of God makes his claims binding in a way that an inert idol cannot sustain.
3.15: en tō legesthai, Sēmeron ean tēs phōnēs autou akousēte, mē sklērynēte tas kardias hymōn hōs en tō parapikrasmō — "As it has just been said: Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion" (Ps 95:7-8, repeated): the repetition of the Psalm citation (cf. vv7-8) within the same argument is rhetorical — the "today" is held open by the repeated utterance. Each reiteration of the warning extends the window of opportunity. The structure of the Greek conditional (ean + subjunctive) is the third-class condition: the hearing is genuinely possible but not guaranteed.
3.16: tines gar akousantes parepikranan? all' ou pantes hoi exelthontes ex Aigyptou dia Mōyseōs — "Who were they who heard and rebelled? Were they not all those Moses led out of Egypt?" (parepikranan: they rebelled, made bitter — aorist indicative): the rhetorical question format drives the analysis of the wilderness failure. The answer is uncomfortable: it was not a fringe minority but the entire generation (all' ou pantes — "was it not all of them?") who had witnessed the exodus. The precedent for the warning is the majority failure of a privileged community — not outsiders who never heard.
3.17: tisi de prosōchthisen tessarakonta etē; ouchi tois hamartēsasin, hōn ta kōla epesen en tē erēmō — "And with whom was God angry for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose bodies perished in the wilderness?" (ta kōla: the limbs, the corpses — G2966, originally meaning "limbs" but used for whole corpses [LXX Num 14:29]): the duration (tessarakonta etē, forty years) appears again; the sustained divine displeasure was matched by the long wilderness sojourn. The physical death — bodies falling in the wilderness — is the external evidence of God's oath enacted.
3.18: tisi de ōmosen mē eiseleusesthai eis tēn katapausin autou ei mē tois apeithēsasin — "And to whom did God swear that they would never enter his rest if not to those who disobeyed?" (apeithēsasin: those who disobeyed — dative plural aorist participle of apeitheō: to refuse to be persuaded, to disobey): Hebrews uses apeitheō (disobedience) as the interpretive gloss for the wilderness failure — the same root as apistia (unbelief, vv12,19): unbelief and disobedience are the same failure seen from different angles. One who refuses to trust God refuses to obey God.
3.19: kai blepomen hoti ouk ēdynēthēsan eiselthein di' apistian — "So we see that they were unable to enter because of their unbelief" (ouk ēdynēthēsan: they were unable — not an external barrier but the incapacity produced by apistia): di' apistian (because of unbelief — the causal dia + accusative): unbelief is not merely a symptom but the operative cause of exclusion. The summary verse gathers the entire Ps 95 exposition: hardening → rebellion → exclusion, all rooted in the single failure of apistia. The connection with the following chapter is seamless: if apistia excluded them, only pistis can ensure entry.

4.12: zōn gar ho logos tou theou kai energēs kai tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos kai diiknoumenos achri merismou psychēs kai pneumatos, harmōn te kai myelōn, kai kritikos enthumsōeōn kai ennoiōn kardias — "for the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit, of joints and of marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart": zōn (living — present active participle: the word has continuous vitality, not static inscription); energēs (active, effective, at work — same root as energeō); tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos (sharper than every two-edged sword — tomōteros: comparative of tomos, sharp): the word's penetrating power exceeds the finest blade. Merismou psychēs kai pneumatos (division of soul and spirit) — not a technical trichotomy but the penetration of the innermost human reality; psychē and pneuma as the two terms for the inner life. Kritikos (discerning, able to judge — the root of krisis): the word not only penetrates but evaluates what it finds.
4.13: kai ouk estin ktisis aphanēs enōpion autou, panta de gymna kai tetrachēlismena tois ophthalmois autou, pros hon hēmin ho logos — "and no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (gymna kai tetrachēlismena: naked and exposed/laid bare — tetrachēlismenā is a NT hapax from trachēlos = neck; the image may be of an animal with its neck bent back for slaughter, fully exposed; or a wrestler pinned with head forced back): the divine omniscience before which all creatures are transparent is the basis of the word's discerning power.
4.15: ou gar echomen archierea mē dynamenon sympathēsai tais astheneiais hēmōn, pepeirasmenon de kata panta kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias — "for we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin" (sympathēsai: to suffer-with, to feel-with — syn + pathein; not merely intellectual comprehension but shared experience): pepeirasmenon (having been tested/tempted — perfect passive participle: permanently in the state of having been tested — the testing is a completed historical event with permanent relevance): kath' homoiotēta (according to likeness, in the same way): the testing was genuinely analogous to human experience. Chōris hamartias (apart from sin): the crucial qualification — tested in every way but without sinning; not "unable to sin" as an ontological constraint but "having withstood every test" as a historical fact.
4.1: phobēthōmen oun mēpote kataleipoménēs epangelias eiselthein eis tēn katapausin autou dokē tis ex hymōn hysterekenai — "Let us therefore be on guard, lest any of you should seem to have fallen short of the promise of entering his rest" (phobēthōmen: aorist passive subjunctive of phobeomai: to be afraid — the aorist hortatory subjunctive calls for a decisive posture of fear/seriousness): kataleipoménēs epangelias (a promise remaining, a promise still open — genitive absolute): the promise has not been voided by Israel's failure; it still stands available. Dokē hysterekenai (seem to have come short — hystereomai: to fall behind, to lack, to miss the mark): the "seem" is not dismissive but reflects the visible, external evidence of falling short.
4.2: kai gar esmen euēngelismenoi kathaper kakeinoi, all' ouk ōphelēsen ho logos tēs akoēs ekeinous, mē synkekeramenous tē pistei tois akousasin — "For we have had the good news proclaimed to us just as they did; but the message they heard was of no value to them, because it was not combined with faith in those who heard it" (euēngelismenoi: perfect passive participle of euangelizō: to proclaim good news — the good news has been proclaimed and stands proclaimed over the community): synkekeramenous tē pistei (mixed/combined with faith — perfect passive participle of synkerannymi: to mix together, to blend; the hearing of the word requires faith-blending to produce nourishment): the metaphor may be dietary — food that is not digested and assimilated provides no benefit. Word without faith fails to profit.
4.3: eiserchometha gar eis [tēn] katapausin hoi pisteusantes — "Now we who have believed are entering that rest" (eiserchometha: present indicative — we are entering, an ongoing process for the believing community): the present tense is significant: entering rest is not entirely future but a present experience of those who believe, alongside its future consummation. Kathōs eirēken, Hōs ōmosa en tē orgē mou, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou (as he has said: As I swore in my anger, They shall never enter my rest): the citation of the oath is now set against the positive declaration — the excluded generation's oath-bound exclusion is the background against which the believing community's entry stands out.
4.4: eirēken gar pou peri tēs hebdomēs houtōs, Kai katepausen ho theos en tē hēmera tē hebdomē apo pantōn tōn ergōn autou — "Somewhere in Scripture God spoke about the seventh day in these words: And God rested on the seventh day from all his works" (Gen 2:2 LXX): the indefinite pou (somewhere) is the same distancing device as in 2:6 — Hebrews cites the divine authority, not the human author. Katepausen (rested — aorist of katapauō: to cause to cease, to rest; the same root as katapausis [rest] throughout): God's own Sabbath rest is the archetype of the rest still on offer. The Gen 2:2 citation imports the cosmic Sabbath into the argument: God's rest is not a past event but an ongoing condition into which the believing may enter.
4.5: kai en toutō palin, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou — "And again in this passage: They shall never enter my rest" (Ps 95:11 repeated): the second citation of the oath, placed immediately after the Gen 2:2 citation, creates the theological juxtaposition: God's rest (Gen 2) has existed since creation; the oath (Ps 95) shows it was not entered by one generation; therefore it still awaits. The brevity of the re-citation signals that the argument has reached its pivot: the two texts together prove that the rest is real, open, and not yet universally possessed.
4.6: epei oun apoleipetai tinas eiselthein eis autēn, kai hoi proteron euangelisthentes ouk eisēlthan di' apeithian — "Therefore it still remains for some to enter that rest, and since those who formerly had the good news proclaimed to them did not enter because of disobedience" (apoleipetai: remains, is left over — present passive; the rest is not exhausted but available): tinas eiselthein (for some to enter) — the indefinite tinas (some, certain ones) marks the future entry as possible but not universal. Di' apeithian (because of disobedience — the same root as in 3:18): disobedience is the cause of exclusion; its opposite (obedience of faith) is the condition of entry.
4.7: palin tina horizei hēmeran, Sēmeron, en Dauid legōn meta tosouton chronon — "God again set a certain day — calling it Today — saying through David much later" (horizei: he sets/defines — from horizō: to mark off a boundary, to determine; the same root as "horizon"): the argument from chronology is precise: David wrote centuries after the exodus generation; the fact that God still speaks the "today" through David proves the rest was not definitively foreclosed in the wilderness but remained open across generations. Sēmeron (Today) — the temporal marker is the key: God keeps reopening the window by repeating the call, which proves the entry remains possible.
4.8: ei gar autous Iēsous katepausen, ouk an peri allēs elalei meta tauta hēmeras — "For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken later about another day" (Iēsous: the Greek form of both Joshua and Jesus — a deliberate ambiguity exploited in the argument): the argument is a second-class conditional (contrary to fact): the "if" implies Joshua's giving of rest did not fulfill the promise. The entry into Canaan was a partial, typological rest — not the ultimate katapausis. The fact that God spoke the "today" through David after Joshua's era proves the Canaan-rest was not the final fulfillment.
4.9: ara apoleipetai sabbatismos tō laō tou theou — "There remains, then, a sabbath-rest for the people of God" (sabbatismos: Sabbath-rest — G4520, a NT hapax; distinct from katapausis used throughout the argument): the change of noun is significant — sabbatismos (Sabbath-keeping, Sabbath-rest) carries the full weight of the weekly Sabbath pattern as a type of the eschatological rest. The Sabbath is not abolished but fulfilled in the rest that remains. Tō laō tou theou (for the people of God): the scope is corporate — the rest belongs to the covenant community, not merely individual souls.
4.10: ho gar eiselthōn eis tēn katapausin autou kai autos katepausen apo tōn ergōn autou, hōsper apo tōn idiōn ho theos — "For anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their own work, just as God rested from his" (katepausen apo tōn ergōn: rested from the works — the same verb and idiom as Gen 2:2 in v4): the logic is typological: God's Sabbath rest (Gen 2) is the pattern; the believer's entry into rest follows the same pattern of ceasing from work. Apo tōn idiōn (from his own works): the idiōn specifies that the rest is from one's own efforts — faith is the opposite of self-effort as the means of entering God's presence.
4.11: spoudasōmen oun eiselthein eis ekeinēn tēn katapausin, hina mē en tō autō tis hypodeigmati pesē tēs apeithias — "Let us make every effort to enter that rest, so that no one will fall by following the same pattern of disobedience" (spoudasōmen: aorist hortatory subjunctive of spoudazō: to hasten, to be zealous, to exert oneself — urgency): the paradox of v11 is noted: one must strive (spoudasōmen) to enter a rest. The effort is not the rest itself but the faith-engagement required to move toward it. Hypodeigmati tēs apeithias (the example/pattern of disobedience): hypodeigma (model, pattern, example) — the wilderness generation's failure is not merely a historical story but a structural pattern to be avoided.
4.14: echontes oun archierea megan dielelythota tous ouranous, Iēsoun ton huion tou theou, kratōmen tēs homologias — "Therefore, since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold firmly to the faith we profess" (dielelythota tous ouranous: perfect active participle of dierchomai: to go through — permanently in the state of having passed through the heavens): the heavenly session is not a static location but the culmination of a journey through the heavens — the Son has traversed the entire cosmic structure and now stands before the Father. Megan archierea (great high priest): the adjective megas marks the superlative quality of this priesthood over all others. Kratōmen tēs homologias (let us hold fast our confession — krateō: to hold firmly, to grip).
4.16: proserchōmetha oun meta parrēsias tō thronō tēs charitos, hina labōmen eleon kai charin heurōmen eis eukairon boētheian — "Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need" (parrēsia: boldness, confidence, freedom of speech — G3954; the right to speak freely before a superior without fear): tō thronō tēs charitos (to the throne of grace): the heavenly throne room, which is the place of judgment in Jewish apocalyptic, is recharacterized by the high priest's presence there as a charis-dispensing court. Eukairon boētheian (timely help, well-timed help — eukairos: opportune, seasonable): the help is not generic but suited to the moment of need.

5.1: pas gar archiereus ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos hyper anthrōpōn kathistatai ta pros ton theon — "for every high priest chosen from among men is appointed to act on behalf of men in relation to God" (ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos: taken from among human beings — the high priest's humanity is a qualification, not a limitation): ta pros ton theon (things toward God — the priestly representative function): the preposition pros indicates directional orientation; the priest stands between human beings and God, facing toward God on their behalf. The two qualifications (v1: chosen from humans; v4: called by God) define the legitimate priesthood.
5.2: metriopathein dynatos tois agnoousin kai planōmenois, epei kai autos perikeitai astheneian — "he can deal gently with the ignorant and wayward, since he himself is beset with weakness" (metriopathein: to moderate one's feelings, to deal with measured emotion — a Stoic philosophical term for the appropriate emotional response between apathy and passion): the high priest's capacity for gentle response (metriopathein) is grounded in his own weakness-experience. The contrast with the Son's sympathy (4:15) is instructive: the Levitical priest sympathizes because he is himself weak; the Son sympathizes because he has been tested.
5.7: hos en tais hēmerais tēs sarkos autou deēseis te kai hiketerias pros ton dynamenon sōzein auton ek thanatou meta kraugēs ischyras kai dakryōn prosenenkas kai eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence" (hiketerias: supplications, olive-branch petitions — the formal term for desperate appeal to a superior; the word is used in Gk papyri for formal petition): the Gethsemane prayer is described without being cited. Eisakoustheis apo tēs eulabeias — "heard because of his reverence" (eulabeia: godly fear, reverent awe — not anxiety but the reverent-careful posture before God): the prayer was heard — not by removal of death but by resurrection through death.
5.8: kaiper ōn huios, emathen aph' hōn epathen tēn hypakoēn — "although he was a Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered" (emathen aph' hōn epathen: learned from/out of what he suffered — the deliberate Greek wordplay on emathen [learned, aorist of manthanō] and epathen [suffered, aorist of paschō]; the rhyme and etymology emphasize the connection between suffering and learning): the learning is not correcting a moral deficiency but the vocational acquisition of lived obedience — the Son did not know obedience by abstract divine knowledge but through the experiential path of incarnate life. Kaiper ōn huios (although being Son): the divine sonship does not exempt from the learning-through-suffering path; it is the Son who walks this path.
5.9: kai teleiōtheis egeneto pasin tois hypakouousin autō aitios sōtērias aiōniou — "and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him" (teleiōtheis: having been made perfect/complete/brought to the goal — aorist passive participle): the perfection (teleiōsis) through suffering and obedience is the high-priestly qualification. Aitios sōtērias aiōniou (cause/source/author of eternal salvation) — the aitios (cause, responsible party) is a philosophical and legal term; the Son is the efficient cause of the salvation that is aiōnios (eternal, age-spanning — not merely long-lasting but of the quality of the coming age).
5.11: peri hou polys hēmin ho logos kai dysermēneutos legein, epei nōthroi gegonate tais akoais — "about this we have much to say, and it is hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing" (nōthroi tais akoais: dull in the ears/hearing — nōthros: sluggish, slow; the same word in 6:12 for sluggishness in faith): the pedagogical self-interruption — the author acknowledges difficulty in communicating and attributes it to the community's spiritual torpor, not the topic's intrinsic inaccessibility. The Melchizedek exposition that follows is difficult not because the truth is obscure but because the community lacks the spiritual attentiveness to receive it.
5.3: kai di' autēn opheilei, kathōs peri tou laou, houtōs kai peri heautou prospherein peri hamartiōn — "This is why he has to offer sacrifices for his own sins as well as for the sins of the people" (opheilei: he is obligated, he owes it — present indicative: the duty is ongoing and inherent to the Levitical priestly role): peri heautou (for himself) — the Levitical high priest's personal sin-offering on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:6, 11) is the defining difference between the Aaronic and Melchizedekian priesthoods. The Son's priesthood, by contrast, does not require self-offering for personal sin (7:27 will make this explicit). The requirement functions here to establish the contrast, not to disparage the Aaronic system.
5.4: kai ouch heautō tis lambanei tēn timēn, alla kaloumenos hypo tou theou, kathōsper kai Aarōn — "And no one takes this honor on himself, but he receives it when called by God, just as Aaron was" (timēn: honor — G5092; also price/value): priestly appointment is by divine call (kaloumenos hypo tou theou — present passive participle: one being called), not self-designation. The Aaron-precedent is the standard: the Aaronic line did not establish itself but was appointed. The negative (ouch heautō: not for himself) establishes the illegitimacy of self-appointment before the positive example.
5.5: houtōs kai ho Christos ouch heauton edoxasen genēthēnai archierea, all' ho lalēsas pros auton, Huios mou ei sy, egō sēmeron gegennēka se — "In the same way, Christ did not take on himself the glory of becoming a high priest. But God said to him: You are my Son; today I have become your Father" (Ps 2:7 LXX): edoxasen genēthēnai archierea (glorified/honored to become high priest): the honor of the high priesthood is expressed as divine glorification. The Ps 2:7 citation establishes the Son's divine appointment as the first of two parallel appointments — sonship first, then priesthood (v6). Sēmeron gegennēka se (today I have begotten you): the eschatological "today" of the resurrection-exaltation is the moment when the divine appointment to sonship is publicly declared (cf. Acts 13:33).
5.6: kathōs kai en heterō legei, Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "And he says in another place: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (Ps 110:4 LXX): en heterō (in another place — a second scriptural location); the two citations (Ps 2:7 + Ps 110:4) together constitute the dual divine appointment: sonship and priesthood. Eis ton aiōna (forever, unto the age): the eternal duration of the Melchizedekian priesthood contrasts with the Aaronic, which ended with the priest's death. Kata tēn taxin Melchisedek (according to the order/rank of Melchizedek — taxis: order, arrangement, rank): not the order as a historical institution but a pattern defined by Melchizedek's profile — priest and king without recorded genealogical succession.
5.10: prosagoreuthteis hypo tou theou archiereus kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "and was designated by God to be high priest in the order of Melchizedek" (prosagoreuthteis: aorist passive participle of prosagoreuō: to address by name, to designate formally — a NT hapax): the formal designation by name/title is the culmination of the preceding argument. The Melchizedek order is first invoked here, then the author declares he has more to say but must pause due to the community's spiritual immaturity (v11). The Melchizedek exposition is deferred to chapters 7-10, with this verse as the bridge.
5.12: kai gar opheilontes einai didaskaloi dia ton chronon, palin chreian echete tou didaskein hymas tina ta stoicheia tēs archēs tōn logiōn tou theou, kai gegonate chreian echontes galaktos kai ou stereās trophēs — "In fact, though by this time you ought to be teachers, you need someone to teach you the elementary truths of God's word all over again. You need milk, not solid food" (dia ton chronon: because of/through the time — the time-span of their discipleship should have produced teachers; the failure is of growth, not initial hearing): stoicheia tēs archēs (elementary principles of the beginning — stoicheia: letters of the alphabet, basic elements; archē: beginning): the ABCs of the faith. Logiōn tou theou (the oracles of God — logion: a divine utterance, oracle): the OT scriptures as the authoritative divine speech. Galaktos (milk) vs. stereās trophēs (solid food): the dietary metaphor for theological development is Hellenistic and Jewish.
5.13: pas gar ho metechōn galaktos apeiros logou dikaiosynēs, nēpios gar estin — "Anyone who lives on milk is still an infant, unskilled in the teaching about righteousness" (apeiros: inexperienced, unskilled — from a-privative + peira: experience; one who has not practiced): logou dikaiosynēs (the word/teaching of righteousness): the genitive is explanatory — the teaching whose content is dikaiosynē. In context, dikaiosynē likely refers to the right-standing and right-conduct that flows from mature Christological understanding, not justification in the Pauline sense. Nēpios gar estin (for he is an infant): nēpios (G3516: infant, one not yet speaking — nē + epos: non-word; one who has no mature speech).
5.14: teleiōn de estin hē sterea trophē, tōn dia tēn hexin ta aisthētēria gegymnasmena echontōn pros diakrisin kalou te kai kakou — "But solid food is for the mature, who by constant use have trained themselves to distinguish good from evil" (teleiōn: of the mature/complete — G5046; the telos-root: those who have reached their purpose): hexis (habit, constant practice — G1838; the state resulting from repeated exercise): the hexis is not a natural aptitude but an acquired state from repeated practice (dia tēn hexin). Aisthētēria (perceptual organs of moral discernment — G145; a philosophical term for the sensory-like faculties of moral perception): the aisthētēria are trained (gegymnasmena: perfect passive — permanently exercised, as an athlete trains) toward diakrisin kalou te kai kakou (discernment of good and evil). The mature discernment of the Melchizedek material requires exactly this kind of trained moral-theological perception.

6.1: dio aphentes ton tēs archēs tou Christou logon epi tēn teleiotēta pherōmetha — "therefore let us leave the elementary doctrine of Christ and go on to maturity" (tēs archēs tou Christou logon: the word of the beginning of Christ — the foundational message; teleiotēta: maturity, completeness — the teleio- root again): aphentes (having left — aorist participle: a completed departure) and pherōmetha (let us be carried, be swept along toward — the passive suggests the Spirit's movement): the author does not call the foundation worthless but insists on building on it rather than always re-laying it. The six foundational items (repentance, faith, baptisms, hand-laying, resurrection, eternal judgment) represent Jewish catechesis that the community has mastered.
6.4: adynaton gar tous hapax phōtisthentas, geusamenous te tēs dōreas tēs epouraniou kai metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou kai kalon geusamenous theou rhēma dynameis te mellontos aiōnos, kai parapersontas, palin anakainizein eis metanoian — "for it is impossible, in the case of those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, and have shared in the Holy Spirit, and have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the age to come, and then have fallen away, to restore them again to repentance" (adynaton: impossible — the strong impossibility claim): the participles describe genuine spiritual experience: phōtisthentas (enlightened — a NT term for baptism), geusamenous (having tasted — genuine experiential contact, not merely intellectual acquaintance), metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou (having become sharers in the Holy Spirit). Parapersontas (having fallen alongside, having fallen away — the para-compound: falling beside the path): the cause of the impossibility (v6): "since they are crucifying once more the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt."
6.13: tō gar Abraam epangeilamenos ho theos, epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai, ōmosen kath' heautou — "for when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself" (epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai: since by no one greater he had to swear — the divine self-swearing is necessitated by the absence of a greater authority): human oaths invoke a greater authority to confirm what is said; God, having no greater authority, swears by his own name. The logic establishes the absolute certainty of the divine promise.
6.17: en hō perissoteron boulomenos ho theos epideixai tois klēronōmois tēs epangelias to ametatheton tēs boulēs autou emesiteusen horkō — "so when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose, he guaranteed it with an oath" (boulomenos: desiring, wishing — deliberate divine will; ametatheton tēs boulēs autou: the unalterableness/immutability of his purpose — ametathetos: unable to be moved from, irrevocable): the divine oath is not required for God's truthfulness but is given for the benefit of the heirs — the double guarantee (promise + oath) gives maximum assurance to those who need confidence.
6.19: hēn hōs ankyran echomen tēs psychēs asphalē te kai bebaian kai eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos — "we have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain" (ankyran tēs psychēs: anchor of the soul — the nautical metaphor of anchor applied to eschatological hope: ancient anchors were dropped into a safe harbor to hold the ship against current and wind; hope in the heavenly sanctuary holds the soul against the currents of the present age): asphalē te kai bebaian (sure and steadfast) — two terms of reliability. Eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos (entering into the inner sanctuary behind the curtain) — hope penetrates the sanctuary barrier where Jesus the forerunner has already entered.
6.2: baptismōn didachēs, epitheseōs te cheirōn, anastaseōs te nekrōn kai kritatos aiōniou — "the teaching about baptisms, the laying on of hands, the resurrection of the dead, and eternal judgment" (baptismōn: the plural is notable — Jewish purification rites used multiple ritual washings, and early Christian instruction had to distinguish Christian baptism from Jewish mikveh, John's baptism, and proselyte baptism): cheirōn epithesis (laying on of hands — a formal gesture of commissioning, blessing, or Spirit-conferral used in both OT and NT); anastaseōs nekrōn and kritatos aiōniou (resurrection of the dead and eternal judgment) are paired eschatological realities — the physical resurrection leads directly to the final assize. The six items form a catechetical curriculum.
6.3: kai touto poiēsomen, eanper epitrepē ho theos — "and God permitting, we will do so" (eanper epitrepē ho theos: if indeed God permits — eanper: third-class condition expressing contingency; epitrepē: subjunctive of epitrepō: to allow, to turn over to): the brief aside acknowledges that the theological progress the author proposes is itself subject to divine permission. The conditional is not a theological hedge about the author's willingness but a genuine acknowledgment that advancing toward maturity requires divine enablement — the same God who commands maturity must also grant the capacity to move toward it.
6.5: kai kalon geusamenous theou rhēma dynameis te mellontos aiōnos — "who have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the coming age" (geusamenous: having tasted — aorist participle; geuomai means genuine experiential contact, not mere surface acquaintance; cf. Ps 34:8 "taste and see that the LORD is good"): kalon rhēma (the goodness/quality of the word): the adjective kalon here is experiential — the word of God proves itself good to those who engage it. Dynameis mellontos aiōnos (powers of the coming age): the miraculous acts performed in the community are advance installments of the age-to-come's power — already operative before the age itself arrives fully.
6.6: kai parapersontas, palin anakainizein eis metanoian, anastaurountas heautois ton huion tou theou kai paradeigmatizontas — "and who have then fallen away — to restore them again to repentance, since they are crucifying once more the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt" (parapersontas: falling alongside/past — NT hapax from parapipto: to fall away, to slip past; the para-prefix suggests falling beside the path): the reason repentance is impossible is stated in the two participial clauses: anastaurountas (crucifying again — ana-: again, anew) and paradeigmatizontas (publicly exposing to shame — from paradeigma: an example displayed publicly). To pursue "restoration" to repentance would be to treat the first repentance as insufficient, implying Christ's sacrifice needs repeating.
6.7: gē gar hē piousa ton ep' autēs erchomenon pollakis hueton, kai tiktousa botanēn eutheton ekeinois di' hous kai geōrgeitai, metalambanei eulogias apo tou theou — "for land that drinks in the rain often falling on it and that produces a crop useful to those for whom it is farmed receives the blessing of God" (piousa: having drunk — aorist participle; the land's reception of rain is a completed act leading to production; tiktousa botanēn eutheton: producing fit/useful vegetation — euthetos: suited to purpose, well-fitted): the agricultural metaphor: the same rain (the same word, the same gifts of the Spirit) falls on both land types; the difference is not in the rain but in the land's response. The rain does not discriminate.
6.8: ekpherouса de akanthas kai tribolous adokimos kai kataras engys, hēs to telos eis kausin — "but land that produces thorns and thistles is worthless and in danger of being cursed. In the end it will be burned" (adokimos: failing the test, rejected, disqualified — from a-privative + dokimazō: to test and approve): land that fails to produce the expected crop fails the test of its purpose; kataras engys (near a curse — not yet cursed, but on the verge): the "nearness" language is merciful — it is not certain doom but an imminent danger. To telos eis kausin (the end toward burning): the burning is the agricultural clearing that allows a new planting cycle — judgment with the possibility of renewal, not just annihilation.
6.9: pepeismetha de peri hymōn, agapētoi, ta kreissona kai echomena sōtērias, ei kai houtōs laloumen — "even though we speak like this, dear friends, we are convinced of better things in your case — the things that belong to salvation" (pepeismetha: we are persuaded — perfect passive of peithō: the author stands in a state of settled persuasion about his community; the perfect tense conveys an abiding conviction): agapētoi (beloved — a direct address that shifts the emotional register after the severe warning): the turn signals that the warning is not a verdict but a pastoral alert issued to those the author expects to remain faithful. Echomena sōtērias (holding to/touching salvation — echomai: to cling to, to hold onto): the things that accompany salvation.
6.10: ou gar adikos ho theos epilathesthai tou ergou hymōn kai tēs agapēs hēs enedeixasthe eis to onoma autou, diakonēsantes tois hagiois kai diakonountes — "for God is not unjust so as to overlook your work and the love that you have shown for his name in serving the saints, as you still do" (ou gar adikos ho theos: for God is not unjust — the divine justice is invoked as the ground of certainty; the community's persevering service cannot go unrewarded because that would be unjust): epilathesthai (to forget — aorist infinitive of epilanthanomai: the divine forgetting is what is ruled out): diakonēsantes... kai diakonountes (having served... and still serving — the aorist + present combination: past service complete and continuing present service).
6.11: epithumomen de hekaston hymōn tēn autēn endeiknysthai spoudēn pros tēn plērophorian tēs elpidos achri telous — "we want each of you to show this same diligence to the very end, so as to make your hope sure" (epithumomen: we desire/long for — not merely "we want" as a casual preference but earnest longing [epithumia = passionate desire]): plērophorian tēs elpidos (full assurance of hope — plērophoria: full carrying through, complete persuasion, assurance that fills): hope is not uncertain wishing but confident expectation that must be maintained with the same diligence that produced past service. Achri telous (until the end): the endpoint is eschatological — the goal is not just present faithfulness but sustained faith to the consummation.
6.12: hina mē nōthroi genēsthe, mimētai de tōn dia pisteōs kai makrothymias klēronountōn tas epangelias — "so that you will not be sluggish, but will imitate those who through faith and patient endurance inherit what has been promised" (nōthroi: sluggish, dull, slow — the same word used in 5:11 for dullness of hearing; here it describes the spiritual torpor that causes one to stop before the finish line): mimētai (imitators — from mimeomai: to imitate, to model oneself on): dia pisteōs kai makrothymias (through faith and long-suffering patience — makrothymia: long-tempered endurance, the opposite of short-tempered capitulation under pressure): faith and makrothymia are the two instruments by which the promised inheritance is received.
6.14: legōn, Ei mēn eulogōn eulogēsō se kai plēthynōn plēthynō se — "saying: I will surely bless you and give you many descendants" (Gen 22:17 LXX): eulogōn eulogēsō and plēthynōn plēthynō — the doubled infinitive-verb construction translates the Hebrew infinitive absolute (bāraḵ ʾăbāreḵeḵā): in the LXX, this idiom intensifies the verb to express absolute certainty and completeness. The translation "I will surely bless / I will surely multiply" captures this but flattens the rhetorical force of the reduplication that signals an oath-level commitment.
6.15: kai houtōs makrothymēsas epetachen tēs epangelias — "and so after waiting patiently, Abraham received what was promised" (makrothymēsas: having endured patiently — aorist participle of makrothymeō: the same makrothymia root of v12): Abraham exemplifies the faith-and-patience combination the community is called to imitate. Epetachen tēs epangelias (received, attained the promise — epetynchanō: to hit the mark, to obtain, to attain): the verb implies directed movement toward a goal that is successfully reached.
6.16: anthrōpoi gar kata tou meizonos omnuousin, kai pasēs autois antilogias peras eis bebaiosin ho horkos — "for people swear by someone greater than themselves, and the oath ends all dispute by confirming what is said" (kata tou meizonos omnuousin: they swear by the greater — the invocation of a superior authority [God, or the emperor, or the gods] to back the claim): antilogias peras (end of contradiction/dispute — antilogia: counter-speaking, contradiction): the oath's social function is to terminate debate by placing a claim under divine sanction. Eis bebaiosin (for confirmation — bebaios: legally reliable, secure): the oath makes the statement legally secure.
6.18: hina dia duo pragma ametatheton, en hois adynaton pseudesthai theon, ischyran paraklēsin echōmen hoi kataphygontes kratēsai tēs prokeimenēs elpidos — "so that by two unchangeable things, in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us" (adynaton pseudesthai theon: it is impossible for God to lie — the same adynaton of 6:4; what is impossible for apostates to recover is paralleled by what is impossible for God to do, anchoring the impossibility on God's character): kataphygontes (having fled to refuge — the image of the one seeking sanctuary, the city of refuge): the community has already fled to God's promise; they need encouragement to remain, not to flee again.
6.20: hopou prodromos hyper hēmōn eisēlthen Iēsous, kata tēn taxin Melchisedek archiereus genomenos eis ton aiōna — "where our forerunner, Jesus, has entered on our behalf. He has become a high priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (prodromos: forerunner, one who runs ahead to prepare the way — a NT hapax; the term was used for advance scouting parties and for the first fruits of the harvest): Jesus is not merely the door to the sanctuary but the first to enter it, paving the way for others to follow the same path. Eis ton aiōna (forever) — the permanence of the Melchizedekian priesthood is the guarantee that the anchor holds permanently.

7.2: hō kai dekatēn apo pantōn emerisen Abraam, prōton men hermēneuomenos basileus dikaiosynēs, epeita de kai basileus Salēm, ho estin basileus eirēnēs — "and to him Abraham apportioned a tenth part of everything. He is first, by translation of his name, king of righteousness, and then he is also king of Salem, that is, king of peace" (basileus dikaiosynēs: king of righteousness — Melchizedek = melek [king] + tsedeq [righteousness]): the etymology-based typology: the name's meaning is part of the type's significance. Basileus Salēm = king of Shalom (peace) — two royal qualities (righteousness and peace) that characterize the Son's rule (cf. Ps 72:1-3, 7; Isa 9:6-7).
7.3: apator, amētor, agenealogētos, mēte archēn hēmerōn mēte zōēs telos echōn — "without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life" (apator, amētor, agenealogētos: without father, without mother, without genealogy — three privative adjectives): the crucial interpretive note: these predicates apply not to Melchizedek's biographical reality but to what the biblical text says about him. The Genesis narrative provides no genealogy, no birth, no death for Melchizedek — the text's silence is treated as typologically significant, not as historical information. Aphomoiōmenos de tō huiō tou theou (made like the Son of God) — Melchizedek is made-like the Son, not the reverse; the Son is the archetype, Melchizedek the type.
7.11: Ei men oun teleiōsis dia tēs Leuitikēs hierōsynēs ēn... ti eti chreia kata tēn taxin Melchisedek heteron anistasthai hierea — "now if perfection had been attainable through the Levitical priesthood... what further need would there have been for another priest to arise according to the order of Melchizedek?" (teleiōsis: perfection, completion — bringing to the goal; the subjunctive ēn in a contrary-to-fact conditional: perfection was not attained through the Levitical system): the argument from the existence of Ps 110:4 — if the Levitical system sufficed, God would not have promised a different kind of priest. The existence of the promise proves the insufficiency of the existing system.
7.16: hos ou kata nomon entolēs sarkikēs gegonen alla kata dynamin zōēs akatalytou — "who has become a priest, not on the basis of a legal requirement concerning bodily descent, but by the power of an indestructible life" (entolēs sarkikēs: fleshly/bodily commandment — the Levitical requirement of physical descent from Aaron; dynamin zōēs akatalytou: the power of an indestructible life — akatalutos: unable to be dissolved, indestructible; zōē not merely biological life but the life of the resurrection-age): the Melchizedekian priesthood is grounded in a different ontological category — not hereditary biology but the indestructible life that overcame death.
7.22: kata tosouton kai kreittōnos diathēkēs gegonen engyos Iēsous — "this makes Jesus the guarantor of a better covenant" (engyos: guarantor, surety, pledge-person — the one who puts himself up as security for another's debt or obligation; a legal-commercial term): Jesus is the covenant's guarantor, not merely its mediator. Kreittōnos diathēkēs (of a better covenant) — the first use of the "better covenant" language that will be elaborated in ch8. Diathēkē can mean both "covenant" (Hebraic-LXX usage) and "will/testament" (Greek usage); Hebrews exploits the ambiguity in 9:15-17.
7.25: hothen kai sōzein eis to panteles dynatai tous proserchomenous di' autou tō theō, pantote zōn eis to entynchanein hyper autōn — "consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them" (eis to panteles: to the uttermost, completely, for all time — panteles combines the ideas of completeness and duration): the interceding high priest who always lives is the basis of the complete salvation — his permanence guarantees the completeness of the benefit. Entynchanein hyper autōn (to intercede for them — entynchanō: to meet/approach someone on behalf of another; the present infinitive indicates continuous intercession).
7.26: toioutos gar hēmin eprepen archiereus, hosios, akakos, amiantos, kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn, kai hypsēloteros tōn ouranōn genomenos — "for it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens" (hosios: devout, holy in relation to God; akakos: without evil, guileless; amiantos: undefiled, unstained; kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn: having been separated from sinners — the perfect participle indicates the permanent separation accomplished at the exaltation): the five adjectives form an ascending scale of purity culminating in the spatial exaltation above the heavens — the highest conceivable point of separation from the earthly-sinful realm.
7.1: houtos gar ho Melchisedek, basileus Salēm, hiereus tou theou tou hypsistou, ho synantēsas Abraam hypostrephonti apo tēs kopēs tōn basileōn kai eulogēsas auton — "this Melchizedek was king of Salem and priest of God Most High. He met Abraham returning from the defeat of the kings and blessed him" (Gen 14:17-20 summarized): basileus Salēm, hiereus tou theou tou hypsistou — the dual title (king + priest) is the essential characteristic that makes Melchizedek typologically significant; no Levitical priest held both offices. Tou theou tou hypsistou (of God Most High — El Elyon): a divine title that transcends ethnic particularism — Abraham recognized the God whom Melchizedek served as the same God he himself worshipped (Gen 14:22).
7.4: theōreite de pēlikos houtos, hō kai dekatēn Abraam edōken ek tōn akrothiniōn ho patriarchēs — "just think how great he was: the patriarch Abraham gave him a tenth of the plunder" (theōreite pēlikos houtos: consider how great this one is — pēlikos: how great in magnitude, how impressive; theōreite: present imperative — ongoing contemplation): the argument from Abraham's action: that ho patriarchēs (the patriarch — the founding father of the covenant people) gave a tithe to Melchizedek establishes Melchizedek's superiority over Abraham, and thus over all who descended from Abraham, including the Levitical priests. Akrothiniōn (top of the pile, best of the spoils): the tithe is of the prime portion, not the leftovers.
7.5: kai hoi men ek tōn huiōn Leui tēn hierōsynēn lambanontes entolēn echousin apodekatouto ton laon kata ton nomon, tout' estin tous adelphous autōn, kai per exelēlythōtas ek tēs osphyos Abraam — "now the law requires the descendants of Levi who become priests to collect a tenth from the people — that is, from their fellow Israelites — even though they also are descended from Abraham" (kata ton nomon: according to the law — the Levitical tithe is a legal requirement, not a voluntary act of homage): the irony noted: the Levitical priests collect a tithe from their own kinsmen-in-Abraham, yet Melchizedek — outside the Abrahamic line's priestly structure — received a tithe from Abraham himself. The tithe-giver is acknowledged as the lesser party.
