7.6: ho de mē genealogoumenos ex autōn dedekatōken Abraam, kai ton echonta tas epangelias eulogēken — "this man, however, did not trace his descent from Levi, yet he collected a tenth from Abraham and blessed him who had the promises" (mē genealogoumenos ex autōn: not being traced in genealogy from them — the Levitical legitimacy rests on genealogical belonging; Melchizedek has no such genealogy and yet exercises superior priestly authority): dedekatōken (has tithed — perfect: the tithing stands as a permanent historical fact with ongoing typological force): ton echonta tas epangelias (the one who has the promises — Abraham, the promise-holder): Melchizedek blessed the one who held the covenant promises, establishing his superiority over the promise-holder himself.
7.7: chōris de pasēs antilogias to elatton hypo tou kreittonos eulogeitai — "and without doubt the lesser is blessed by the greater" (chōris pasēs antilogias: without any dispute/contradiction — antilogia: counter-word, objection): the logical axiom: the one who blesses stands in a position of superiority over the one blessed. Since Melchizedek blessed Abraham, Melchizedek is by definition the greater of the two. The axiom is presented as universally acknowledged — chōris pasēs antilogias closes off potential objections.
7.8: kai hōde men dekatous apothnēskontes anthrōpoi lambanousin, ekei de martyroumenos hoti zē — "in the one case, the tenth is collected by people who die; but in the other case, by him who is testified to be living" (apothnēskontes anthrōpoi: people who die — present participle: dying people collect tithes in the Levitical system; their mortality is an ongoing condition that limits the system's permanence): martyroumenos hoti zē (testified to be living — the passive martyroumenos: Scripture testifies of Melchizedek's living status by recording no death): the silence of the text about Melchizedek's death is treated as a positive testimony to his living — not biographical speculation but typological reading of textual silence.
7.9: kai hōs epos eipein, di' Abraam kai Leui ho dekatous lambanōn dedekatōtai — "one might even say that Levi, who collects the tenth, paid the tenth through Abraham" (hōs epos eipein: so to speak, if one may put it this way — a hedging expression used in classical Greek to introduce a bold or paradoxical statement without full assertion): the author acknowledges the argumentative leap with hōs epos eipein. Dedekatōtai (has been tithed — perfect passive): the argument rests on the concept of corporate solidarity — Levi's existence within Abraham's loins makes Levi participant in Abraham's actions. This is a Semitic concept of solidarity that the author applies to the genealogical-typological argument.
7.10: eti gar en tē osphyi tou patros ēn, hote synantēsen autō Melchisedek — "because when Melchizedek met Abraham, Levi was still in the body of his ancestor" (en tē osphyi tou patros: in the loins of the father/ancestor — osphys: loin, the reproductive seat in biblical anthropology): the argument assumes a corporate and physical understanding of generational solidarity: Levi was "present" in Abraham in the same sense that Isaac and Jacob were latent in Abraham's biological seed. This is the same logic by which Paul argues that all humanity participated in Adam's sin (Rom 5:12). The typological argument does not require the author to commit to Levi's personal awareness but to the representative structure of ancestry.
7.12: metatithemenēs gar tēs hierōsynēs ex anankēs kai nomou metathesis ginetai — "for when the priesthood is changed, the law must be changed also" (metatithemenēs: being changed, being transferred — present passive participle of metatithēmi: to transfer, to remove from one place/condition to another; the same root as ametatheton in 6:17-18): the logical connection between priesthood and law: the Mosaic Torah is structurally organized around the Levitical priesthood (sacrificial law, purity law, the entire cultic apparatus). If the priesthood is superseded, the law itself is reorganized — not annulled but reorganized around the new priesthood of the Melchizedekian type.
7.13: eph' hon gar legetai tauta phylēs heterias metesche, aph' hēs oudeis proseschēken tō thysiastēriō — "he of whom these things are said belonged to a different tribe, and no one from that tribe has ever served at the altar" (phylēs heterias metesche: he shared in/belonged to a different tribe — heteros: another of a different kind; the tribe of Judah, not Levi): the factual observation that Jesus descended from Judah is the historical grounding for the argument. Tō thysiastēriō (the altar): the Levitical altar-service was tribe-specific — Judah had no standing there. The Son's priesthood must therefore be of a different order.
7.14: prodēlon gar hoti ex Iouda anatetalken ho kyrios hēmōn, eis hēn phylēn peri hiereon Mōysēs ouden elalēsen — "for it is clear that our Lord descended from Judah, and in regard to that tribe Moses said nothing about priests" (prodēlon: openly evident, perfectly clear — the descent from Judah is not disputed): anatetalken (has arisen/risen — perfect of anatellō: to rise as the sun rises, to spring up as a plant springs from the earth; the messianic "branch" language of Isa 11:1, Jer 23:5, Zech 3:8 uses this root): the perfect tense indicates a permanent risen state. Peri hiereon Mōysēs ouden elalēsen (Moses said nothing about priests in regard to that tribe): the Torah's silence about Judah as a priestly tribe is itself evidence that the Son's priesthood operates outside Mosaic categories.
7.15: kai perissoteron eti katadēlon estin, ei kata tēn homoiotēta Melchisedek anistatatai hiereus heteros — "and this is even more clear if another priest like Melchizedek arises" (perissoteron eti katadēlon: more abundantly yet evident — intensive comparison; katadēlon: thoroughly evident, transparent): the double comparison (perissoteron + eti) emphasizes that the Melchizedek argument makes what was already clear even more transparent. Kata tēn homoiotēta Melchisedek (according to the likeness of Melchizedek): the "likeness" (homoiotēs) echoes 4:15 — the same word used for Christ's likeness to human beings in testing; here the structural-priestly likeness to Melchizedek.
7.17: martyreitai gar hoti Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "for it is declared: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (Ps 110:4 cited again): martyreitai (it is testified, it is declared — present passive: Scripture continues to testify, present tense): the Psalm testimony is not a past utterance but a continuing divine declaration. The citation anchors the entire priesthood-argument: the "forever" (eis ton aiōna) is the explicit scriptural warrant for the indestructible-life priesthood argument of v16.
7.18: athetēsis men gar ginetai proagousēs entolēs dia to autēs asthenēs kai anōpheles — "for on the one hand there is a setting aside of a former commandment because of its weakness and uselessness" (athetēsis: setting aside, annulment — from atheteō: to put away as if it did not exist, to declare null; a legal term): the language is precise about what has been set aside: not the entire Mosaic revelation but the specific entolē (commandment, regulation) concerning Levitical-bodily qualification. Asthenēs kai anōpheles (weak and useless): the Levitical system is not wrong or evil — it is asthenēs (weak, incapable of achieving its stated goal of perfection) and anōpheles (not profitable in the ultimate sense).
7.19: ouden gar eteleiōsen ho nomos, epeisagōgē de kreittōnos elpidos, di' hēs engizomen tō theō — "for the law made nothing perfect, and a better hope is introduced through which we draw near to God" (ouden eteleiōsen ho nomos: the law brought nothing to its goal — the telos-root: the law could not bring the worshiper to the telos of the relationship it promised): epeisagōgē (bringing in alongside/in addition — the prefix epi + eis: a supplementary introduction): the better hope is introduced not as a rejection of what preceded but as the fulfillment of what the law pointed toward. Engizomen tō theō (we draw near to God): the goal of the entire priestly system — access to God — is now achieved through the better hope.
7.20: kai kath' hoson ou chōris horkōmosia, hoi men gar chōris horkōmosias eisin hiereis gegonotes — "and it was not without an oath! Others became priests without any oath" (horkōmosia: oath-taking, the swearing of an oath — a compound noun appearing only in Hebrews in the NT): the contrast is between the Levitical ordination (governed by Torah regulation without a personal divine oath) and the Melchizedekian appointment (grounded in the divine oath of Ps 110:4). The oath is the second element that makes Jesus's priesthood superior: not only is it of a different order and not only is it eternal, but it is divinely oath-secured.
7.21: ho de meta horkōmosias dia tou legontos pros auton, Ōmosen Kyrios kai ou metamelēthēsetai, Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna — "but he became a priest with an oath through the one who said to him: The Lord has sworn and will not change his mind: You are a priest forever" (Ps 110:4): ou metamelēthēsetai (will not repent/change his mind — metamelomai: to have an after-care, to regret and reverse; the divine negative: God does not experience post-oath regret): the oath-formula emphasizes both the irrevocability of the appointment (sworn) and the immutability of the commitment (will not change his mind). The juxtaposition with the mortal-priest succession of v23 makes the contrast stark.
7.23: kai hoi men pleiones eisin gegonotes hiereis, dia to thanatō kōluesthai paramenein — "now there were many of those priests, since death prevented them from continuing in office" (kōluesthai paramenein: being prevented from continuing — kōlyō: to hinder, to obstruct; paramenō: to remain alongside, to continue in place): the plurality of Levitical priests is not a mark of the system's strength but of its structural limitation — each priest's death necessitated a successor, creating an inherently interrupted ministry. The many-priests problem is the death-problem: mortality prevents the continuity that effective priesthood requires.
7.24: ho de dia to menein auton eis ton aiōna aparabaton echei tēn hierōsynēn — "but because Jesus lives forever, he has a permanent priesthood" (aparabaton: not passing to another, permanent, inviolable — a NT hapax; from a-privative + parabainō: to step past/across): aparabatos means the priesthood cannot be crossed over to another, cannot be transgressed or bypassed; it is perpetual and non-transferable. The word combines the senses of permanence (it does not end) and exclusivity (it cannot be handed to another). Dia to menein auton eis ton aiōna (because of his abiding forever): the indestructible life (v16) is the ontological ground of the non-transferable priesthood.
7.27: hos ouk echei kath' hēmeran anankēn, hōsper hoi archiereis, proteron hyper tōn idiōn hamartiōn thysias anapherein, epeita tōn tou laou; touto gar epoiēsen ephapax heauton anenengkas — "unlike the other high priests, he does not need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for his own sins, and then for the sins of the people. He sacrificed for their sins once for all when he offered himself" (kath' hēmeran: according to the day, day by day — the dailiness of the Levitical system is its limitation; not even the annual high-priestly atonement suffices for the accumulation): ephapax (once for all) — the single completed offering is the answer to the daily necessity. Heauton anenengkas (having offered himself — reflexive: the high priest and the sacrifice are the same person, a structural impossibility in Levitical law): the self-offering resolves what no animal sacrifice could — the need for a priest without sin to offer a sacrifice sufficient once for all.
7.28: ho nomos gar anthrōpous kathistēsin archieras echontas astheneian, ho logos de tēs horkōmosias tēs meta ton nomon huion eis ton aiōna teteleiōmenon — "for the law appoints as high priests men in all their weakness; but the oath, which came after the law, appointed the Son, who has been made perfect forever" (kata ton nomon: according to the law, governed by the law; echontas astheneian: having weakness — the weakness is not moral infirmity alone but the structural weakness of mortality and its consequences): ho logos tēs horkōmosias (the word of the oath-taking) — the Ps 110:4 oracle is a logos (a word with authority) expressed as an horkōmosia (oath). Teteleiōmenon eis ton aiōna (having been made perfect/complete forever — perfect passive participle): the Son's perfection through suffering (2:10, 5:9) is now permanent; the word "forever" closes the argument of chapters 5-7.

8.1: kephalaion de epi tois legomenois, toiouton echomen archierea, hos ekathisen en dexia tou thronou tēs megalōsynēs en tois ouranois — "now the point in what we are saying is this: we have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven" (kephalaion: the main point, summary, head of the argument — not merely a summary but the key point that the whole argument has been building toward): tēs megalōsynēs (of the Majesty): a circumlocution for God, common in Jewish reverence-language. Ekathisen (sat down — aorist: the completed seating): the seated high priest, the completed work.
8.5: hoitines hypodeigmati kai skia latreuousin tōn epouraniōn, kathōs kechrēmatistai Mōysēs mellōn epitelein tēn skēnēn — "who serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. For when Moses was about to erect the tent, he was instructed by God, saying: See that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain" (hypodeigmati kai skia: copy/example and shadow — both terms indicate derivativeness: the earthly sanctuary is not the original but its earthly reproduction; kechrēmatistai: has been warned/instructed by divine oracle — the same verb used for Joseph being warned in a dream, Matt 2:12): the divine instruction proves the earthly sanctuary is intentionally modeled on a heavenly reality.
8.6: nyn de diaphorōteras tetychēken leitourgias, hosō kai kreittōnos estin diathēkēs mesitēs, hētis epi kreittōsi epangeliais nenomothetētai — "but as it is, Christ has obtained a ministry that is as much more excellent than the old as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises" (diaphorōteras leitourgias tetychēken: has obtained a more excellent ministry — diaphoros: different, superior; mesitēs: mediator, middle-person between two parties; nenomothetētai: has been legislated/established — the perfect passive indicates the settled legal establishment of the new covenant): the comparative structure: as much better as the covenant is better, as much as the promises are better — a chain of comparatives.
8.8: mempthomenos gar autous legei, Idou, hēmerai erchontai, legei Kyrios — "for he finds fault with them when he says: Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord. (mempthomenos autous: finding fault with them — the "them" are the people who made the first covenant inadequate by their failure): the author's reading of Jer 31 is that the new covenant promise itself implies a critique of the old: God would not promise a new covenant if the old had been without fault. The Jeremiah citation frames the promise as a divine critique-and-solution to the first covenant's demonstrated inadequacy.
8.9: hoti ouk estai kata tēn diathēkēn hēn epoiēsa tois patrasin autōn en hēmera epilabomenou mou tēs cheiros autōn exagagein autous ek gēs Aigyptou, hoti autoi ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē mou, kagō ēmelēsa autōn, legei Kyrios — "not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt. For they did not continue in my covenant, and so I showed no concern for them" (ouk enemeinan en tē diathēkē: they did not remain in the covenant — the first covenant's failure is not God's failure of design but Israel's failure of faithfulness; kagō ēmelēsa autōn: and I showed no concern for them — the divine response is corresponding covenant-withdrawal).
8.13: en tō legein Kainēn pepalaiōken tēn prōtēn; to de palaioumenon kai gēraskon engys aphanismou — "in speaking of a new covenant, he makes the first one obsolete. And what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away" (pepalaiōken: has made old — perfect active: the declaration of newness accomplished the obsolescence of the old): the kainēn (new — qualitatively new, not just recent) is the key: when Scripture uses kainēn, it implies the oldness of what it replaces. Engys aphanismou (near disappearance) — the present tense palaioumenon and gēraskon (becoming old, aging) suggest the process was underway at the time of writing; the Jerusalem temple's destruction (70 CE) would be the visible completion.
8.2: tōn hagiōn leitourgos kai tēs skēnēs tēs alēthinēs, hēn epēxen ho kyrios, ouk anthrōpos — "who serves in the sanctuary, the true tabernacle set up by the Lord, not by a mere human being" (leitourgos: liturgist, one who performs public religious service — a priestly/cultic technical term; tōn hagiōn leitourgos: liturgist of the holy things/sanctuary): tēs skēnēs tēs alēthinēs (the true tabernacle — alēthinēs: true, genuine, the original of which others are copies): the alēthinos/alēthēs distinction: alēthinos means "genuine, real, as opposed to copies and shadows" rather than merely "truthful." The earthly tabernacle is a copy; this one is the original. Hēn epēxen ho kyrios, ouk anthrōpos (which the Lord pitched, not a human being): the divine construction establishes its nature — not constructed within the created order.
8.3: pas gar archiereus eis to prospherein dōra te kai thysias kathistatai; hothen anagkaion echein ti kai touton ho prospherē — "every high priest is appointed to offer both gifts and sacrifices, and so it was necessary for this one also to have something to offer" (eis to prospherein: for the purpose of offering — the infinitive of purpose defines the essential function of the high priest): anagkaion (necessary): the logical necessity follows from the universal: since every high priest must offer, and since Christ is a high priest, Christ must also have an offering. The offering cannot be the repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical system — his offering is identified as his own self (9:14, 26).
8.4: ei men oun ēn epi gēs, oud' an ēn hiereus, ontōn tōn kata nomon proscheron ōra ta dōra — "if he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are priests who offer the gifts prescribed by the law" (ei men oun ēn epi gēs: if indeed he were on earth — second-class conditional, contrary to fact: the present condition is that Christ is NOT on earth): the argument is about jurisdictional categories: the earthly sphere already has its established priestly order. Christ's priesthood operates in the heavenly sphere — not competing with the Levitical system on its own terrain but fulfilling the reality of which the earthly is only the copy.
8.7: ei gar hē prōtē ekeinē ēn amemptos, ouk an deuteras ezēteito topos — "for if there had been nothing wrong with that first covenant, no place would have been sought for another" (ei... ēn amemptos: if it had been blameless — second-class conditional, contrary to fact; amemptos: blameless, without fault): the argument from the existence of Jer 31's new covenant promise: God would not promise what was already provided. The existence of the promise is itself evidence of the first covenant's inadequacy. Amemptos does not imply the first covenant was wrong or evil — but that it was incomplete and incapable of achieving the final goal.
8.10: hoti autē hē diathēkē hēn diathēsomai tō oikō Israēl meta tas hēmeras ekeinasmeta tas hēmeras ekeinasdidonai nomous mou eis tēn dianoian autōn, kai epi kardias autōn epigrapsō autous, kai esomai autois eis theon kai autoi esontai moi eis laon — "I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be my people" (Jer 31:33 LXX): didonai nomous eis tēn dianoian (giving laws into the mind — dianoia: the faculty of thinking, the mind as the organ of understanding): and epi kardias epigrapsō (I will write upon their hearts — epigrapsō: to write upon, to engrave on): the new covenant's law is internalized — not external tablets but internal inscription. Dianoia (mind/understanding) and kardia (heart) together cover the whole inner person. The covenant formula esomai autois eis theon... autoi... eis laon (I will be to them as God, they will be to me as a people) is the bedrock covenantal language from the Sinai tradition — the new covenant fulfills the same relational goal.
8.11: kai ou mē didaxōsin hekastos ton politen autou kai hekastos ton adelphon autou legōn, Gnōthi ton Kyrion, hoti pantes eidēsousin me apo mikrou heōs megalou autōn — "no longer will they teach their neighbor, or say to one another, Know the Lord, because they will all know me, from the least of them to the greatest" (ou mē... didaxōsin: they shall by no means teach — the double negative ou mē + aorist subjunctive is the strongest Greek negation): Gnōthi ton Kyrion (Know the Lord): in the first covenant, external instruction mediated the knowledge of God through priests and prophets; in the new covenant, the knowledge is direct and universal. Pantes eidēsousin me (all shall know me — oida: the perfect-present knowledge that is settled and complete): from the smallest to the greatest — the knowledge transcends social and educational stratification.
8.12: hoti hileōs esomai tais adikiais autōn kai tōn hamartiōn autōn ou mē mnēsthō eti — "for I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more" (Jer 31:34 LXX): hileōs esomai (I will be merciful/gracious — hileōs: propitious, gracious, disposed to forgive; the word used for the Day of Atonement mercy): ou mē mnēsthō eti (I will by no means remember any longer — double negative ou mē + aorist subjunctive + eti: the most emphatic possible negation): the divine "forgetting" is not cognitive failure but an act of deliberate covenant release — God will not bring up the forgiven sins as legal evidence. This is the culminating promise of the new covenant: the basis of everything that precedes it in the Jeremiah citation is this final act of complete forgiveness.

9.5: hyperanō de autēs Cheroubein doxēs kataskiazonta to hilastērion — "above it were the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat" (Cheroubein doxēs: cherubim of glory — the genitive doxēs is a genitive of quality: cherubim characterized by glory, glory-cherubim; kataskiazonta: overshadowing, spreading wings over — the cherubim whose wings spread over the mercy seat [Exod 25:20] are the guardians of the divine presence): hilastērion: the mercy seat (kappōret) where the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement — the place of atonement/propitiation. The same word in Rom 3:25 describes Christ as the hilastērion.
9.7: eis de tēn deuteran hapax tou eniautou monos ho archiereus, ou chōris haimatos, ho prospherei hyper heautou kai tōn tou laou agnoēmatōn — "but into the second only the high priest goes, and he but once a year, and not without taking blood, which he offers for himself and for the unintentional sins of the people" (hapax tou eniautou: once of the year, once per year — the hapax (once) anticipates the contrast with Christ's single sacrifice: the Levitical once-per-year vs. the Christological once-for-all; agnoēmatōn: unintentional sins, inadvertent transgressions — from agnoeo, to be ignorant of; the Levitical atonement covered inadvertent sins specifically, raising the question of intentional sins).
9.11: Christos de paragenomenos archiereus tōn mellontōn agathōn dia tēs meizōnos kai teleiotera skēnēs ou cheiropioētou, tout' estin ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs — "but when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and more perfect tent not made with hands, that is, not of this creation, he entered once for all into the holy places" (tōn mellontōn agathōn: of the good things that have come/are coming — the manuscript tradition is divided between mellontōn [coming/future] and genomenōn [having come/past]; both are theologically defensible): ou cheiropioētou (not made by hands — a technical term for that which belongs to a different ontological order, not constructed by human craft): ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs (not of this creation) — the parenthetical identification of the greater tent as belonging to a different created order from the earthly sanctuary.
9.12: oude di' haimatos tragōn kai moschōn dia de tou idiou haimatos eisēlthen ephapax eis ta hagia aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos — "he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (ephapax: once for all — the compound of epi + hapax, emphasizing the finality and non-repeatability; aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos: having found/secured eternal redemption — lytrōsis: the act of ransoming/redeeming, from lytron = ransom price): the contrast is between the animal blood that enables the annual temple ritual and the self-blood that enables the once-for-all heavenly entry. The perfect result (heuramenos = having found, secured) is permanent.
9.15: kai dia touto diathēkēs kainēs mesitēs estin, hopōs thanatou genomenou eis apolutrōsin tōn epi tē prōtē diathēkē parabaseōn, tēn epangelian labōsin hoi keklēmenoi tēs aiōniou klēronomias — "therefore he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant" (apolutrōsin tōn... parabaseōn: redemption-from transgressions — the apo-compound: ransom that moves someone away from the enslaving situation; parabaseōn: transgressions, willful crossings of the boundary, deliberate violations — in contrast to the agnoēmatōn of v7): the death redeems backward (from transgressions under the first covenant) as well as forward (securing the eternal inheritance).
9.22: kai schedon en haimati panta katharizatai kata ton nomon kai chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis — "indeed, under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (schedon: almost, nearly — the qualification acknowledges that a few Levitical purifications used water or fire rather than blood [Num 31:23; Lev 15:10-11]; the general principle holds): chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis (without blood-shedding, forgiveness does not occur): haimatekcysia (blood-shedding, bloodshed): a rare compound found only here in the NT; the rule is absolute within the framework of the argument — atonement requires life given (Lev 17:11).
9.24: ou gar eis cheiropioēta eisēlthen hagia Christos, antitypa tōn alēthinōn, all' eis auton ton ouranon, nyn emphanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou hyper hēmōn — "for Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf" (antitypa tōn alēthinōn: antitypes/copies of the true things — antitypon: the corresponding impression, the copy-form that corresponds to its type): the terminology is inverted from usual typological language: normally the OT type corresponds to the NT antitype; here the earthly sanctuary is the antitypon (the copy, the corresponding-form) of the heavenly original. Nyn emphanisthēnai (now to appear — the present emphasis): Christ is currently appearing in God's presence for the community's benefit.
9.28: houtōs kai ho Christos hapax prosenechtheis eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias, ek deuterou chōris hamartias ophthēsetai tois auton apekdechomenois eis sōtērian — "so Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him" (hapax prosenechtheis: having been offered once — the once-for-all offering is complete; eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias: to bear the sins of the many — anaphero: to carry up, to bear [as a sacrifice]: Isa 53:12 LXX uses anaphero for the Servant bearing the sins of many): apekdechomenois (eagerly waiting — the same word as Rom 8:19, 23; the eschatological expectation-posture of the community awaiting the parousia).
9.1: eiche men oun hē prōtē dikaiōmata latreias to te hagion kosmikon — "now the first covenant had regulations for worship and also an earthly sanctuary" (dikaiōmata latreias: regulations/ordinances of worship — dikaiōmata: righteous requirements, legally binding ordinances; the word used in Rom 8:4 for what the Spirit fulfills): to hagion kosmikon (the earthly/worldly sanctuary — kosmikon: belonging to the kosmos, the material world-order): the modifier kosmikon immediately flags the earthly sanctuary as belonging to the temporal, material sphere — as opposed to the heavenly sanctuary of ch8. The contrast is built into the description from the first word.
9.2: skēnē gar kateskeuasthē hē prōtē en hē hē te lychnia kai hē trapeza kai hē prothesis tōn artōn, hētis legetai Hagia — "a tabernacle was set up. In its first room were the lampstand and the table with the consecrated bread; this was called the Holy Place" (lychnia: the lampstand — the golden menorah of Exod 25:31-40; trapeza kai hē prothesis tōn artōn: the table and the setting-out of the loaves — the table of showbread, Exod 25:23-30, prothesis: the setting-forward, the presentation, the public display): Hagia (Holy Place — the first room, accessible to all priests): the inventory is not merely descriptive but sets up the contrast with what is beyond the second curtain. Each piece of furniture pointed beyond itself to the divine presence.
9.3: meta de to deuteron katapetasma skēnē hē legomenē Hagia Hagiōn — "behind the second curtain was a room called the Most Holy Place" (meta to deuteron katapetasma: after/behind the second curtain — katapetasma: the hanging veil/curtain; the same word used for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion in the Synoptics): Hagia Hagiōn (Most Holy Place — superlative construct, lit. Holy of Holies; the Hebrew qodesh qodashim): the spatial structure of the tabernacle embodies a theology of graduated holiness — the outer court, the Holy Place, the Most Holy Place — with increasingly restricted access that Hebrews reads as a pointer to the restricted access of the old covenant.
9.4: chrysoun echousa thymiasētrion kai tēn kibōton tēs diathēkēs periekekkalymmēnēn pantothen chrysiō, en hē stamnos chrysē echousa to manna kai hē rhabdos Aarōn hē blastēsasa kai ai plakes tēs diathēkēs — "this room had the golden altar of incense and the gold-covered ark of the covenant. Inside the ark were the golden jar of manna, Aaron's staff that had budded, and the stone tablets of the covenant" (thymiasētrion: the incense altar or incense-vessel — there is scholarly discussion on whether this refers to the altar of incense or the censer; Hebrews may be associating the incense-altar with the Most Holy Place for theological purposes since the incense was burned in connection with the high priest's entry): rhabdos Aarōn hē blastēsasa (the staff of Aaron that budded — Num 17): the three contents of the ark (manna, Aaron's staff, the covenant tablets) are theologically significant — providential sustenance, divine vindication of priesthood, and the covenant law. Each points to what the new covenant fulfills.
9.6: toutōn de houtōs kateskeuasmenōn, eis men tēn prōtēn skēnēn dia pantos eisiasin hoi hiereis tas latreias epitelountes — "when everything had been arranged like this, the priests entered regularly into the outer room to carry on their ministry" (dia pantos: continually, at all times — the constant repetition of priestly ministry in the outer room): tas latreias epitelountes (accomplishing/performing the priestly services — epitelountes: present participle, ongoing completion): the daily priestly ministry in the outer room (lampstand tending, incense, showbread) was regular and repeated. The contrast with the inner room (once per year) structures the argument about access and proximity to the divine presence.
9.8: touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou, mēpō pephanerōsthai tēn tōn hagiōn hodon, eti tēs prōtēs skēnēs echousēs stasin — "the Holy Spirit was showing by this that the way into the Most Holy Place had not yet been disclosed as long as the first tabernacle was still standing" (touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou: the Holy Spirit making this clear — dēloō: to make manifest, to reveal; the Spirit is the author of the tabernacle's theological meaning): mēpō pephanerōsthai (not yet to have been made manifest — perfect passive infinitive: the perfect indicates the sustained non-disclosure): the Spirit intentionally encoded the limitation of access into the tabernacle's structure as a theological statement: the way into God's presence was blocked, not because the first tabernacle was temporary but because the sacrifice that opens the way had not yet been made.
9.9: hētis parabolē eis ton kairon ton enestēkota — "this is an illustration for the present time" (parabolē: parable, type, illustration — from para + ballō: to throw alongside; a placing-beside-for-comparison): the tabernacle is explicitly called a parabolē, and the "present time" (kairon ton enestēkota) is the time of the new covenant's inauguration. The tabernacle's function as parable/type was always its primary theological function. Kata syneidēsin (in relation to conscience — syneidēsis: the shared-knowledge/conscience, the inner moral awareness): the Levitical gifts and sacrifices could not achieve the cleansing that matters — the inner moral-spiritual conscience — only external ceremonial status.
9.10: monon epi brōmasin kai posasin kai diaphorois baptismois, dikaiōmata sarkos mechri kairou diorthōseōs epikeimenoi — "they are only a matter of food and drink and various ceremonial washings — external regulations applying until the time of the new order" (dikaiōmata sarkos: ordinances/regulations of flesh — sarx here = the bodily/material dimension; fleshly ordinances as opposed to the spiritual transformation that conscience requires): mechri kairou diorthōseōs (until the time of the setting-right/correction — diorthōsis: a setting-upright, a reformation, a restoration to the correct condition; only here in the NT): the Levitical system was designed with a temporal limit — mechri (until): its validity was always provisional, awaiting the kairos (the appointed time) of the new order.
9.13: ei gar to haima tragōn kai taurōn kai spodos damaleos rhantizouśa tous kekoinōmenous hagiaizei pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta — "if the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sprinkled on those who are ceremonially unclean sanctify them for the purification of the flesh" (spodos damaleos: ashes of a heifer — the red heifer ritual of Num 19; the ashes mixed with water were sprinkled on those defiled by corpse-contact): kekoinōmenous (those having been defiled — perfect passive: their defilement is an established condition; koinoō: to make common, to defile by contamination): pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta (for the cleanness of the flesh): the Levitical purification achieves external, ritual cleanliness — the body is made ceremonially fit for worship — but does not address the moral-spiritual interior.
9.14: posō mallon to haima tou Christou, hos dia pneumatos aiōniou heauton prosēnegken amōmon tō theō, katharizei tēn syneidēsin hymōn apo nekrōn ergōn eis to latreuein theō zōnti — "how much more, then, will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God" (posō mallon: how much more — the a minori ad maius argument [if the lesser, how much more the greater]): dia pneumatos aiōniou (through the eternal Spirit — aiōniou: belonging to the coming age, eternal): the Spirit's involvement in the self-offering is not merely instrumental but constitutive of its quality — the sacrifice transcends the temporal order by virtue of the Spirit's eternal character. Heauton prosēnegken amōmon (he offered himself unblemished — the reflexive: the priest and the offering are one; amōmos: without spot or blemish, the sacrificial requirement): katharizei tēn syneidēsin (cleanses the conscience — syneidēsin): where the Levitical blood achieved external cleansing, Christ's blood reaches the syneidēsis — the inner moral awareness.
9.16: hopou gar diathēkē, thanaton anankē pheresthai tou diathemenou — "for where there is a will/covenant, the death of the one who made it must be established" (diathēkē: will/testament; also covenant — the Greek word covers both): here Hebrews exploits the Greek legal sense of diathēkē as a last will and testament, which only takes effect at death. Thanaton anankē pheresthai (it is necessary for a death to be brought forward/established — pheresthai: to be borne, produced as evidence; the passive suggests a legal presentation of proof): the death of the testator is the condition for the will's validity. This legal logic is applied to the covenant: the new covenant's benefits require the death of the covenant-maker.
9.17: diathēkē gar epi nekrois bebaia, epei mēpote ischyei hote zē ho diathemenos — "because a will is in force only when somebody has died; it never takes effect while the one who made it is living" (epi nekrois bebaia: confirmed/secure over/upon the dead — bebaia: legally firm, secure; same bebaios root used throughout for reliability): the legal observation is the argumentative bridge: the old covenant was dedicated with blood at Sinai (v18-20), and the new covenant is inaugurated through the death of its mediator. Both covenants require death for their validity.
9.18: hothen oude hē prōtē chōris haimatos enkekainistai — "this is why even the first covenant was not put into effect without blood" (enkekainistai: has been inaugurated, dedicated — perfect passive of enkainizō: to inaugurate, to make new, to consecrate by a fresh act; the kaino-root: the same word group as kainē diathēkē [new covenant]): the irony is noted: the first covenant was enkainizō-d (inaugurated/made new) with blood, and the new covenant is likewise inaugurated with the blood of its mediator. The blood-inauguration pattern is continuous across both covenants.
9.19: lalētheisēs gar pasēs entolēs kata ton nomon hypo Mōyseōs panti tō laō, labōn to haima tōn moschōn kai tōn tragōn meta hydatos kai eriou kokkinou kai hyssōpou auto te to biblion kai panta ton laon errantisen — "when Moses had proclaimed every commandment of the law to all the people, he took the blood of calves, together with water, scarlet wool and branches of hyssop, and sprinkled both the scroll and all the people" (Exod 24:6-8 with some elaboration): Hebrews includes details not in the Exodus narrative (calves and goats, water, scarlet wool, hyssop) — possibly drawing on Num 19 or later Jewish tradition. Auto te to biblion (both the scroll itself): the sprinkling of the scroll is not in Exod 24 — Hebrews may be indicating that the written law itself was consecrated with blood, showing the intimate connection between covenant-document and covenant-blood.
9.20: legōn, Touto to haima tēs diathēkēs hēs eneteilato pros hymas ho theos — "saying: This is the blood of the covenant, which God has commanded you to keep" (Exod 24:8 LXX): Touto to haima tēs diathēkēs (This is the blood of the covenant): the formula that Jesus reuses at the Last Supper (Matt 26:28; Mark 14:24; Luke 22:20; 1 Cor 11:25) — the verbal link is deliberate and Hebrews' readers would recognize it. The Sinai blood-of-the-covenant becomes the interpretive lens for the crucifixion event; the new covenant's blood-dedication recapitulates and surpasses the Sinai pattern.
9.21: kai tēn skēnēn de kai panta ta skeuē tēs leitourgias tō haimati homoiōs errantisen — "in the same way, he sprinkled with the blood both the tabernacle and everything used in its ceremonies" (homoiōs: in the same way, likewise): the tabernacle's blood-dedication extended to all its vessels and furnishings. Hebrews' summary of Levitical inauguration practice goes beyond Exod 24 — the Mosaic consecration of the tabernacle (Exod 40) and its furnishings apparently involved blood in tradition, though the Exodus narrative focuses on anointing oil. The pattern is: everything in the old covenant system had its existence mediated through blood.
9.23: anankē oun ta men hypodeigmata tōn en tois ouranois toutois katharizesthai, auta de ta epourania kreittosi thysiais para tautas — "it was necessary, then, for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these sacrifices, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these" (kreittosi thysiais: better sacrifices — plural): the plural is unexpected since Christ offered one sacrifice. Hebrews uses the plural rhetorically to match the comparison: since the earthly needed purification with multiple sacrifices, the heavenly required "better" (single but superior) ones. Alternatively, the plural may encompass all aspects of Christ's sacrificial work. Anankē (necessity): the purification of the heavenly things is necessary because the heavenly sanctuary is the one where Christ presents his self-offering — it must be purified to function as the ultimate Holy of Holies.
9.25: oud' hina pollakis prospherē heauton, hōsper ho archiereus eiserchetai eis ta hagia kat' eniauton en haimati allotriō — "nor did he enter heaven to offer himself again and again, the way the high priest enters the Most Holy Place every year with blood that is not his own" (kat' eniauton: year by year, annually — the annual cycle of Day of Atonement entry): en haimati allotriō (with blood belonging to another — allotrios: belonging to another person, foreign, not one's own): the contrast is precise: the Levitical high priest must bring someone else's blood because his own would be unfit; Christ enters with his own blood because he is both priest and sacrifice. The repetition of entry reveals the incompleteness; the once-for-all entry declares the completion.
9.26: epei edei auton pollakis pathein apo katabolēs kosmou; nyn de hapax epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn eis athetēsin tēs hamartias dia tēs thysias autou pephanerōtai — "otherwise Christ would have had to suffer many times since the creation of the world. But he has appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to do away with sin by the sacrifice of himself" (epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn: at the completion/end of the ages — synteleia: joint-completion, the convergence of all the ages at their end-point): the incarnation and sacrifice are placed at the hinge-point of history — the ages converge on this event. Eis athetēsin tēs hamartias (for the setting-aside of sin — athetēsis: the same legal annulment term used in 7:18 for the setting-aside of the former commandment): sin is legally annulled, not merely covered over.
9.27: kai kath' hoson apokeitai tois anthrōpois hapax apothansein, meta de touto krisis — "just as people are destined to die once, and after that to face judgment" (apokeitai: it is laid away/stored up/appointed — from apokeimai: what is reserved and waiting; death is humanity's standing appointment): hapax apothansein (to die once): the human "once" of death mirrors the divine "once" of Christ's sacrifice in v28 — the typological pairing is the rhetorical point: just as humanity dies once and faces judgment once, so Christ was offered once and will appear once for salvation. The universal human pattern (one death, one judgment) is the frame for the particular divine act (one sacrifice, one appearing).

10.1: skian gar echōn ho nomos tōn mellontōn agathōn, ouk autēn tēn eikona tōn pragmatōn — "for since the law has but a shadow of the good things to come instead of the true form of these realities" (skian echōn: having/holding a shadow — the shadow is the law's characteristic possession, not an accidental quality; ouk autēn tēn eikona: not the image itself — the law has a shadow but not the eikona [image, form, replica] of the things): the distinction between skia (shadow) and eikōn (image) is that the shadow lacks the substance and clarity of the image — both are less than the reality, but the image is a truer representation than the shadow. The Platonic background of copy-and-original is mediated here through Hellenistic Jewish thought.
10.4: adynaton gar haima taurōn kai tragōn aphairein hamartias — "for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins" (adynaton: impossible — the same strong impossibility as 6:4; the repeated adynaton is characteristic of Hebrews' categorical claims): the impossibility is ontological, not merely pragmatic — animal blood cannot remove moral guilt because the ontological gap between animal life and human guilt is unbridgeable. The repeated OT sacrifices implicitly acknowledged this — if they had achieved the removal, they would not need repeating (v2).
10.5: sōma de katērtisō moi — "but a body you have prepared for me" (katērtisō: you prepared, fashioned, made ready — perfect active, addressing God): the LXX of Ps 40:6 reads sōma de katērtisō moi where the MT has ōzen koritha li (ears you have dug/opened for me — the idiom for God opening the ears for obedience). The LXX translator may have understood the opened ears as a metonymy for the receptive body (the obedient self), or may have been working from a variant Hebrew text. Hebrews uses the LXX reading to make the incarnation the fulfillment of Ps 40 — the Son comes in the prepared body to do God's will.
10.10: en hō thelēmati hēgiasmenoi esmen dia tēs prosphoras tou sōmatos Iēsou Christou ephapax — "and by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all" (hēgiasmenoi esmen: we have been sanctified — perfect passive periphrastic: the sanctification is a completed state with present reality; ephapax: once for all — the definitive, non-repeatable character of the offering): the thelemati (by the will) links back to Ps 40:8 ("to do your will, O God") — the Son's will-doing is the mechanism of the community's sanctification. The body-offering that replaced the animal-sacrifices is the will-doing that the Psalm anticipated.
10.11: kai pas men hiereus hestēken kath' hēmeran leitourgōn kai tas autas pollakis prospherōn thysias, haitines oudepote dynantai perielein hamartias — "and every priest stands daily at his service, offering repeatedly the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins" (hestēken: stands — the standing priest is the image of unfinished work; contrast with the seated Son of v12): the Levitical priests' standing is not incidental but structural — there were no chairs in the sanctuary, because the work was never done. The contrast with the seated Christ (who sat down after offering one sacrifice for sins forever) makes the posture theologically significant.
10.19: echontes oun, adelphoi, parrēsian eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn en tō haimati Iēsou — "therefore, brothers and sisters, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus" (parrēsian: confidence, bold access, freedom of speech/entry — in the classical world: the citizen's right to speak in the assembly; in this context: the right to enter the sanctuary that was previously restricted): eisodon (entry, access) — the parrēsia is specifically an entry-right, not only an emotional confidence. The blood of Jesus is the basis of the access right, as the high priest's blood was the basis of his Day of Atonement access.
10.23: katechōmen tēn homologian tēs elpidos aklinē, pistos gar ho epangeilamenos — "let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful" (katechōmen: let us hold down, hold fast — same verb as 3:6, 14: the community's grip on what was received; homologian: confession — same word as 3:1; the community's public declaration of faith; aklinē: without bending, without inclining — rigid against pressure): the reason to hold fast is divine faithfulness, not human effort — the community holds its grip because the one who promised is trustworthy.
10.25: mē enkataleipontes tēn episynagōgēn heautōn, kathōs ethos tisin, alla parakalontes, kai tosoutō mallon hosō blepete engizousan tēn hēmeran — "not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near" (episynagōgēn: assembling together — a compound of syn- + agōgē: the bringing-together of the group; also used in 2 Thess 2:1 for the eschatological gathering): the present tense participle indicates ongoing neglect by some within the community. Hēmeran (the Day): the eschatological Day of the Lord/Christ's return, whose proximity motivates intensified mutual encouragement.
10.26: hekousiōs gar hamartanontōn hēmōn meta to labein tēn epignōsin tēs alētheias, ouketi peri hamartias apoleipetai thysia — "for if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" (hekousiōs: willingly, voluntarily, deliberately — the contrast with the Levitical atonement of inadvertent sins [9:7, agnoēmatōn]; the deliberate, willful rejection of Christ's sacrifice leaves no atonement mechanism available): epignōsin tēs alētheias (the full knowledge of the truth) — this is not ordinary ignorance but knowledge-with-recognition of what has been received.
10.31: phoberon to empesein eis cheiras theou zōntos — "it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God" (phoberon: fearful, terrifying — predicate adjective; empesein eis cheiras: to fall into the hands of — the idiom of helpless subjection to a superior power): the formulation inverts the Psalter's comfort-language of "falling into God's hands" as divine mercy (2 Sam 24:14 = falling into God's hands rather than human hands). Here the falling is judgment rather than mercy — the distinction being the attitude of the one who falls.
10.38: ho de dikaios mou ek pisteōs zēsetai, kai ean hyposteilētai, ouk eudokei hē psychē mou en autō — "but my righteous one shall live by faith, and if he shrinks back, my soul has no pleasure in him" (ek pisteōs zēsetai: will live from/out of faith — the same Hab 2:4 citation as in Rom 1:17 and Gal 3:11, but with different emphasis: Hebrews adds the negative counterpart "if he shrinks back"): hyposteilētai (shrinks back — aorist subjunctive of hypostellō: to draw back, shorten sails, retreat): the nautical imagery again — shrinking back is the failure to hold one's position, the pulling-back of the one who was moving forward.
10.2: epei ouk an epausanto prospheromenai, dia to mēdemian echein eti syneidēsin hamartiōn tous latreuontas hapax kekathrismenous — "for if they could have, would they not have stopped being offered? Because the worshippers, once truly cleansed, would no longer have been conscious of their sins" (syneidēsin hamartiōn: consciousness/conscience of sins — syneidēsis: the inner moral awareness, the shared-knowing of the self with itself; the consciousness of sin is the awareness that the guilt-problem remains unresolved): the argument is from silence converted to speech: the continued repetition of sacrifices is itself evidence of their incompleteness — if they had truly resolved sin, there would be nothing left to repeat. Hapax kekathrismenous (having been cleansed once): the hapax here is the goal never achieved under the old system but achieved by Christ.
10.3: all' en autais anamnēsis hamartiōn kat' eniauton — "instead, in those sacrifices there is an annual reminder of sins" (anamnēsis hamartiōn: a remembrance/memorial of sins — anamnēsis: active recalling, not passive memory; the word used in the eucharistic tradition [Luke 22:19; 1 Cor 11:24-25] for the remembrance-unto-presence that the Lord's Supper effects): the annual sacrifice functions as an anamnēsis of sins — a formal re-membering that the sin problem persists, an annual re-presentation of the guilt before God. Contrast with 8:12: the new covenant promise is that sins will be remembered no more (ou mē mnēsthō eti).
10.6: holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk eudokēsas — "in burnt offerings and offerings for sin you took no pleasure" (Ps 40:6 LXX, continued): ouk eudokēsas (you did not take pleasure — aorist: a completed act of divine non-pleasure): the divine dissatisfaction is not with the obedience that offered these sacrifices but with the inadequacy of animal sacrifice as a vehicle for the relational-moral restoration God desires. The Psalm reads God's heart as desiring obedience over sacrifice (cf. Mic 6:6-8; Hos 6:6) — a prophetic tradition that Hebrews enlists to show the Levitical system was always interim.
10.7: tote eipon, Idou hēkō, en kephalidi bibliou gegraptai peri emou, tou poiēsai, ho theos, to thelēma sou — "then I said, See, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written about me in the scroll of the book" (Ps 40:7-8 LXX): en kephalidi bibliou (in the head/roll of the book — kephalis: scroll-roller, the end of the scroll where the title was; or the beginning/heading of the scroll): the Son identifies himself as the one about whom the entire scriptural scroll is written — not merely a verse here or there but the whole. Tou poiēsai... to thelēma sou (to do your will): the Son's incarnation is defined as will-doing — he comes not to perform animal sacrifices but to perform the will of God that the sacrifices symbolized.
10.8: anoteron legōn hoti Thysias kai prosphoras kai holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk ēthelēsas oude eudokēsas, haitines kata nomon prospherontai — "when he said above, You have not desired nor taken pleasure in sacrifices and offerings and burnt offerings and sin offerings — which are offered in accordance with the law" (kata nomon prospherontai: offered according to the law — the parenthetical clarification that these are the very offerings the Torah prescribes): the argument is careful: God commanded the Levitical system through Moses, yet through the Psalms God also says he does not desire those same offerings. The resolution is that the commandments governed the pedagogical interim; the Psalm reveals the heart that the system was always pointing toward.
10.9: tote eirēken, Idou hēkō tou poiēsai to thelēma sou; anairei to prōton hina to deuteron stēsē — "then he added, Behold, I have come to do your will. He removes the first covenant order in order to establish the second" (anairei to prōton: he removes/takes away the first — anairei: present tense, actively removing): anairō has the sense of lifting up and taking away — the same word used for executing/removing someone (Acts 2:23, 5:33). The removal of the first is the act that creates space for the second. Hina to deuteron stēsē (so that he might establish the second — aorist subjunctive: a completed, decisive establishment): the two covenants cannot coexist permanently; the establishment of the second requires the removal of the first.
10.12: houtos de mian hyper hamartiōn prospheras thysian eis to diēnekes ekathisen en dexia tou theou — "but this priest, having offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, sat down at the right hand of God" (mian thysian: one sacrifice — the numerical contrast with the many; eis to diēnekes: in perpetuity, continuously, for all time — diēnekes: carried through without break, uninterrupted; a mathematical/administrative term for a continuous running total): ekathisen: sat down — the single aorist that the entire argument builds toward. The sitting is the posture of completed work; no chair in the sanctuary because no Levitical work was ever complete. The seated Son marks the ending of a priestly service that is not repeated.
10.13: to loipon ekdechomenos heōs tethōsin hoi echthroi autou hypopodion tōn podōn autou — "waiting from that time until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet" (ekdechomenos: waiting, expecting — present active participle: the Son's current activity is expectant waiting): the posture pairs sitting (completed sacrifice) with waiting (expected consummation). The session is not passive inactivity but active intercession (7:25) combined with sovereign expectancy. Hypopodion tōn podōn autou (footstool for his feet — Ps 110:1): the language of absolute dominion; the footstool-enemy is the one fully subdued under the victor's feet in ancient Near Eastern victory imagery.
10.14: mia gar prosphora teteleiōken eis to diēnekes tous hagiazomenous — "for by a single offering he has brought to completion for all time those who are being made holy" (teteleiōken: he has brought to completion, perfected — perfect active: the perfecting stands as a completed act with ongoing results): tous hagiazomenous: those being sanctified — present passive participle: the community's sanctification is an ongoing process, but it rests on the completed perfecting act of the one offering. The perfect teteleiōken + present hagiazomenous holds together the finished work and the ongoing application.
10.15: martyrei de hēmin kai to pneuma to hagion — "the Holy Spirit also bears witness to us concerning this" (martyrei: bears witness, testifies — present tense: the Spirit continues to testify through Scripture): the testimony is ongoing — not merely a past utterance but a present testimony that reaches the community through its reading of the OT. The Spirit who spoke through Jeremiah continues to speak the same word. Hēmin (to us): the first-person plural signals that the scriptural testimony is not merely historical but addressed to the present community.
10.16: hautē hē diathēkē hēn diathēsomai pros autous meta tas hēmeras ekeinaslegeicurios: didonai nomous mou epi kardias autōn, kai epi tēs dianoias autōn epigrapsō autous — "This is the covenant I will make with them after those days, says the Lord: I will put my laws on their hearts and write them on their minds" (Jer 31:33, repeated from 8:10): the repetition of the Jer 31 citation is not redundant but deliberate — it frames the argument about Christ's one sacrifice with the promise of the new covenant's complete internalized forgiveness. Epi kardias... epi tēs dianoias (on hearts... on minds): heart and mind together represent the whole inner person; the law is inscribed on both the affective and cognitive dimensions.
10.17: kai tōn hamartiōn autōn kai tōn anomiōn autōn ou mē mnēsthō eti — "I will remember their sins and their lawless deeds no more" (Jer 31:34 LXX, final clause): ou mē mnēsthō eti: the emphatic double-negative + aorist subjunctive + eti (still/any longer) — the absolute divine non-recollection. The citation concludes the long scriptural argument: the new covenant achieves what the Levitical system could not — the permanent resolution of the sin-problem through God's deliberate covenant-forgetting. The citation is placed after the description of Christ's completed sacrifice to show: the sacrifice achieves what the Jeremiah promise required.
10.18: hopou de aphesis toutōn, ouketi prosphora peri hamartias — "where there is forgiveness of these sins, there is no longer any offering for sin" (aphesis: forgiveness, release — the letting-go of the debt/obligation): the logical conclusion drawn from the Jer 31 citation: if sins are no longer remembered by God, no mechanism remains for addressing them. Ouketi prosphora peri hamartias: no longer an offering for sin — the sacrificial system becomes structurally unnecessary once its goal has been achieved. This verse is the pivot from the doctrinal argument (chs 7-10:18) to the parenetic application (10:19-39).
10.20: hēn enekainisen hēmin hodon prosphaton kai zōsan dia tou katapetasmatos, tout' estin tēs sarkos autou — "by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh" (prosphaton: freshly slaughtered, new — from pros + sphattō: the freshly-killed; prosphatos could mean both newly-made and freshly-sacrificed, which is significant for the sacrificial context): zōsan (living): the way is living because the one who opened it is alive; the death through which access was gained was not the end of the story. Tou katapetasmatos, tout' estin tēs sarkos autou (the curtain, that is, his flesh): the identification of the curtain with Christ's flesh is the interpretive move — the curtain in the tabernacle separated the holy from the most holy; Christ's body was the barrier between humanity and God's presence that his death opened.
10.21: kai hierea megan epi ton oikon tou theou — "and since we have a great high priest over the house of God" (hierea megan: a great priest — the superlative epithet echoing 4:14; epi ton oikon tou theou: over the house of God): the "house of God" picks up the oikos-language of ch3 (Moses faithful in the house; Christ faithful over the house). The community is both the house and the assembled worshippers who now have access to what the house symbolized.
10.22: proserchōmetha meta alēthinēs kardias en plērophoria pisteōs, errantismenoi tas kardias apo syneidēseōs ponēras kai leloumenoi to sōma hydati katharō — "let us draw near with a sincere heart and in full assurance of faith, with our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water" (proserchōmetha: let us draw near — hortatory subjunctive echoing 4:16; the same verb for priestly approach to the sanctuary): errantismenoi tas kardias (hearts having been sprinkled — perfect passive participle: the sprinkling has already occurred and its state remains): the language maps Old Covenant purification rites onto the inner person: the heart is the locus of the sprinkling. Leloumenoi to sōma hydati katharō (bodies washed with pure water — also perfect passive): the two perfects (sprinkled hearts, washed bodies) describe the completed state that enables the drawing-near.
10.24: kai katanōomen allēlous eis paroxysmon agapēs kai kalōn ergōn — "and let us consider how to spur one another on to love and good works" (katanōomen: let us observe carefully, consider attentively — same verb as 3:1 [consider Jesus]; the verb requires fixing attention on something with intention): paroxysmon: provocation, stimulation, incitement — the noun is from paroxynō: to sharpen to a point, to provoke; the cognate gave us "paroxysm" in English. The provocative stimulation is positive here — goading one another toward love, not toward dispute (Acts 15:39 uses the same word for Paul and Barnabas's sharp disagreement). Agapēs kai kalōn ergōn (love and good works): the two outcomes that the mutual provocation is to produce.
10.27: phobera de tis ekdochē kriseōs kai pyrōs zēlos esthiein mellontos tous hypenantious — "but a terrifying expectation of judgment and a raging fire that will consume the adversaries of God" (ekdochē: expectation, receiving — waiting to receive something; the word implies the thing awaited is already determined and approaching): pyrōs zēlos: a jealousy/fury of fire — zēlos can mean either jealousy (jealous fire) or zealousness (fierce fire); the genitive describes the fire's quality as consuming-zeal. Tous hypenantious: the adversaries, those who stand against — those who reject the Son become adversaries of God.
10.28: athetēsas tis nomon Mōyseōs chōris oiktirmōn epi dysin ē trisin martyrsin apothnēskei — "anyone who rejected the law of Moses died without mercy on the evidence of two or three witnesses" (athetēsas: aorist participle of atheteō: to set aside as if nonexistent, to nullify — the same word used in 7:18 for the setting-aside of the commandment): the conditional-analogical structure: if the lesser (Mosaic law-rejection) carried capital punishment without mercy, how much more the greater? Chōris oiktirmōn: without compassions — oiktirmos: the gut-feeling of compassion, the visceral mercy; the court could not allow emotion to override the Deuteronomic death-penalty requirement (Deut 17:6).
10.29: posō dokite cheironos axióthēsetai timōrias ho ton huion tou theou katapatēsas, kai to haima tēs diathēkēs koinon hēgēsamenos, en hō hēgiasthē, kai to pneuma tēs charitos enybrisas — "how much worse punishment, do you think, will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God, has treated as something common the blood of the covenant by which he was sanctified, and has insulted the Spirit of grace" (katapatēsas: having trampled underfoot — the image of complete contempt, treating what is most holy as street-dirt): koinon hēgēsamenos (having considered common/profane — koinos: what belongs to everyone, thus defiled, unclean): the covenant blood is considered no better than ordinary blood. Enybrisas: having insulted, outraged — hybrizō: to treat with contempt that demeans; the three aorist participles escalate in severity: trampling → profaning → insulting.
10.30: oidamen gar ton eiponta, Emoi ekdikēsis, egō antapodōsō; kai palin, Krinei kyrios ton laon autou — "for we know him who said, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, and again, The Lord will judge his people" (Deut 32:35-36 cited): Emoi ekdikēsis (Vengeance belongs to me — the divine possessive): the citation from the Song of Moses establishes the divine monopoly on recompense. Krinei Kyrios ton laon autou: the Lord judges his people — the judgment is not of outsiders but of the covenant people. The second citation (Deut 32:36) is a warning that the covenant relationship does not insulate from divine judgment but rather intensifies accountability.
10.32: anamimNēskesthe de tas proteron hēmeras, en hais phōtisthentes pollēn athlēsin hypemeinete pathēmatōn — "but recall the earlier days when, after you were enlightened, you endured a hard and painful struggle" (anami mnēskesthe: recall, bring back to memory — present imperative: ongoing recollection as a pastoral resource): phōtisthentes (having been enlightened — aorist passive: the completed event of baptism/conversion; the same word as 6:4): the community's conversion is the reference point. Athlēsin: an athletic contest, a struggle — athleō: to compete athletically; the metaphor frames the community's persecution-experience as a contest requiring both skill and endurance.
10.33: touto men oneidismois te kai thlipsesin theatrizomenoi, touto de koinōnoi tōn houtōs anastrophentōn genēthentes — "sometimes being publicly exposed to reproach and affliction, and sometimes standing alongside those who were treated this way" (theatrizomenoi: being made a spectacle in the theater — theatron: theater, public display; the passive: being put on display for the public's entertainment): the Roman practice of public shaming and spectacle-punishment is the background. Koinōnoi: sharers, partners — the community's solidarity with those suffering was itself an act of faith that incurred risk; identifying with the condemned made one a target.
10.34: kai gar tois desmiois synepathēsate kai tēn harpagēn tōn hyparchontōn hymōn meta charas prosedexasthe — "for you showed compassion to those in prison and joyfully accepted the plundering of your property" (tois desmiois synepathēsate: you suffered-with the prisoners — synpatheō: shared-suffering, co-suffering; the same root as 4:15 [the Son sympathizes with weaknesses]): harpagēn: plundering, seizure — the goods were forcibly taken, not voluntarily surrendered; the community's joy was not in the loss itself but in the enduring possession. Ginōskontes echein heautous kreittōna hyparxin kai menousan (knowing that you yourselves have a better, abiding possession): the consciousness of the better possession transforms the attitude toward the lost one.
10.35: mē apobalēte oun tēn parrēsian hymōn, hētis echei megalēn misthapodosian — "therefore do not throw away your confidence, which carries a great reward" (apobalēte: aorist subjunctive of apoballō: to throw away, to cast off — a single decisive act of discarding): the parrēsia that was given at conversion and maintained through persecution must not be voluntarily discarded at the end. Misthapodosian: recompense, reward — the same word as 2:2 (the law's just misthapodosia); now the positive reward of faithful perseverance rather than the penalty of transgression. The parrēsia is worth keeping because what it carries is of greater value than what one might gain by abandoning it.
10.36: hypomonēs gar echete chreian, hina to thelēma tou theou poiēsantes komizēsthe tēn epangelian — "for you need endurance, so that when you have done the will of God you may receive what was promised" (hypomonēs: endurance, the staying-power under pressure — hypo: under + menō: to remain; the virtue of remaining in place under the weight): komizēsthe: may receive, carry off — komizō: originally to care for and carry; the middle voice suggests personal reception of something belonging to oneself. The endurance must first produce the will-doing, which then receives the promise.
10.37: eti gar mikron hoson hoson, ho erchomenos hēxei kai ou chronizei — "for yet a little while, and the coming one will come and will not delay" (Isa 26:20 + Hab 2:3 combined in LXX): mikron hoson hoson: a very little while — the double hoson intensifies the brevity; a Hebraism for "just a tiny moment"; ho erchomenos: the coming one — the Habakkuk text in the LXX has a person ("he will come") rather than the MT's "it" (the vision); Hebrews follows the LXX and applies the "coming one" to Christ. Ou chronizei: will not delay — chronizō: to linger in time, to be late; the coming is certain and near.
10.39: hēmeis de ouk esmen hypostolēs eis apōleian, alla pisteōs eis peripoiēsin psychēs — "but we do not belong to those who shrink back and are destroyed, but to those who have faith and so preserve their souls" (hypostolēs: shrinking back — genitive; from hypostellō: to take in the sails, to shorten, to draw back; the nautical image of a ship shortening its sails in fear): eis apōleian: unto destruction/loss — the final state of the shrinking-back; pisteōs eis peripoiēsin psychēs (faith unto the preservation/acquisition of the soul — peripoiēsis: the acquiring for oneself, the preservation; from peri + poieō: to make-around, to secure). The community's self-definition is constituted by belonging to the faith-group, not the shrinking-back group.

11.1: Estin de pistis elpizomenōn hypostasis, pragmatōn elenchos ou blepomenōn — "now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" (hypostasis: substance, underlying reality, foundation — the same word as 1:3 [exact imprint of his hypostasis] and 3:14 [if we hold the beginning of our hypostasis]): in Greek philosophy hypostasis referred to the underlying reality beneath appearances; here faith is described as the hypostasis of the hoped-for things — not hope itself but the substantiality, the made-real quality, that faith gives to what is hoped for. Elenchos: proof, demonstration, conviction — a legal/logical term for evidence that compels belief: faith is the demonstration of unseen realities, not a subjective state of optimism.
11.3: Pistei noumen katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou, eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai — "by faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" (katērtisthai tous aiōnas rhēmati theou: the ages were fitted-together/constituted by the word of God — katartizō: to prepare, put in order, constitute): eis to mē ek phainomenōn ta blepomena gegonenai: so that the visible came to be not from visible things — the creation-from-nothing principle (creatio ex nihilo): the visible order does not derive from pre-existing visible material but from the divine word that preceded and generated it.
11.6: chōris de pisteōs adynaton euarestēsai; pisteusai gar dei ton proserchomenon tō theō hoti estin kai tois ekzētousin auton misthapadotēs ginetai — "and without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him" (adynaton euarestēsai: impossible to please — the third adynaton of Hebrews, establishing faith as the absolute prerequisite for approaching God; tois ekzētousin auton: to those who seek him — present active participle: the seeking is ongoing, not a one-time quest): misthapadotēs: rewarder, one who gives back wages — misthos + apodidōmi; a compound found only here in the NT: God repays the investment of faith-seeking with himself.
11.10: exedecheto gar tēn tous themelious echousan polin, hēs technitos kai dēmiourgos ho theos — "for he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God" (technitos: craftsman, artisan — one who designs and makes; dēmiourgos: public craftsman, builder — in Plato the Demiurge is the craftsman-god who orders the cosmos): the city with foundations is the opposite of a tent (the patriarchal dwelling) — permanent, foundationed, divinely constructed. Abraham's tent-dwelling was not failure but faith — he lived in the type while seeking the antitype.
11.13: kata pistin apethanon houtoi pantes, mē labontes tas epangelias, alla pōrrothen autas idontes kai aspasamenoi kai homologēsantes hoti xenoi kai parepidēmoi eisin epi tēs gēs — "these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" (pōrrothen autas idontes: having seen them from far off — the patriarchs' eschatological vision that reached beyond their earthly lifetime; aspasamenoi: having greeted, embraced — the gesture toward something at a distance: a wave, a salute): xenoi kai parepidēmoi: strangers and sojourners/exiles — the technical terms for resident-aliens in Hellenistic usage; the patriarchs self-identify as non-citizens of the present order.
11.24: Pistei Mōysēs megas genomenos ērnēsato legesthai huios thygatros Pharaō — "by faith Moses, when he was grown up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter" (ērnēsato legesthai: refused to be called — the double verb: refusing + the passive infinitive of naming; it was not a name he bore but a name given by others that he rejected): the faith-act is the refusal of a social identity. Egyptian royal adoption made Moses heir to immense privilege; his refusal is faith's voluntary social descent in solidarity with the covenant people.
11.26: meizona plouton hēgēsamenos tōn Aigyptou thēsaurōn ton oneidismon tou Christou — "he considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt" (meizona plouton hēgēsamenos: having considered greater wealth — the deliberate cognitive evaluation of comparative worth; ton oneidismon tou Christou: the reproach of Christ — the identification of Moses's suffering with the Messiah's reproach is the Christological reading of Ps 69:9 and 89:50-51): Moses's reproach is identified with Christ's because it was reproach suffered for the sake of the same covenant promise — a typological solidarity across the testaments.
11.33: hoi dia pisteōs katēgōnisanto basileias, eirgatēsanto dikaiosynēn, epetychon epangeliōn, ephraxan stomata leontōn — "who through faith conquered kingdoms, enforced justice, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions" (katēgōnisanto basileias: conquered kingdoms — the military-athletic verb of wrestling/competing to a finish; the OT judges and kings who won battles): epetychon epangeliōn (obtained promises): not the complete eschatological promise (11:39) but specific historical promises — Gideon's deliverance, David's kingship, etc. Ephraxan stomata leontōn: stopped the mouths of lions — Daniel in the lions' den (Dan 6) and Samson (Judg 14:6).
11.35: hoi de ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin, hina kreittōnos anastaseōs tychōsin — "some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better resurrection" (ou prosdexamenoi tēn apolutrōsin: not having accepted the release/redemption — the same apolutrōsis as 9:15 and Eph 1:14; here not the redemptive act but the offer of release from torture): kreittōnos anastaseōs: a better resurrection — not the resuscitation of 11:35a (returning to mortal life) but the eschatological resurrection to immortal life. The faith that refuses earthly rescue for the sake of the coming resurrection is the supreme expression of 11:1's assurance-of-hoped-for-things.
11.39: kai houtoi pantes martyrēthentes dia tēs pisteōs ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian — "and all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised" (martyrēthentes: having been witnessed to, testified about, approved — the passive of martyreō: to bear witness; God's testimony about them in Scripture is the divine commendation): ouk ekomisanto tēn epangelian (did not receive the promise — the singular promise): the entire OT community of faith died before the fulfillment; they received historical-provisional fulfillments but not the eschatological completion. Tou theou peri hēmōn kreitton ti problepsamenos (v40: God having provided something better for us) — the new covenant community is the completing context for the OT cloud of witnesses.
11.2: en tautē gar emarturēthēsan hoi presbyteroi — "for by this the people of old received their commendation" (emarturēthēsan: were borne witness to, testified about — aorist passive: God's testimony about them came from without, not from their own self-assessment): hoi presbyteroi: the elders, the ancestors — not ecclesiastical officers but the patriarchs and figures of Israel's past. En tautē (by this faith): the antecedent is the definition of faith in v1; the OT saints were commended precisely through this faith-as-substance-and-evidence. Scripture's testimony about them constitutes the divine commendation.
11.4: Pistei pleiona thysian Abel para Kain prosēnenken tō theō, di' hēs emarturēthē einai dikaios, martyrountos epi tois dōrois autou tou theou, kai di' autēs apothanōn eti lalei — "by faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain. Through his faith he was commended as righteous, when God himself testified about his gifts. And by his faith Abel still speaks, even though he is dead" (pleiona thysian: a better/more/greater sacrifice — pleionos: the comparative of greatness, not specifying in what way): the text of Genesis does not explain why Abel's sacrifice was accepted; Hebrews supplies the answer: faith. Eti lalei: still speaks — present tense: Abel's voice continues through Scripture. The blood of Abel cried out from the ground (Gen 4:10); the author of Hebrews reads that continuing cry as the ongoing testimony of faith.
11.5: Pistei Enōch metetethe tou mē idein thanaton, kai ouch heurisketo dioti metethēken auton ho theos — "by faith Enoch was taken from this life so that he did not experience death. He could not be found, because God had taken him away" (Gen 5:24): metetethe: was translated/transferred — aorist passive of metatithēmi: to transfer from one place to another; the same verb used for the transferring of the priesthood in 7:12. Tou mē idein thanaton (so as not to see death — genitive articular infinitive of result): the translation bypassed death entirely — a unique testimony to faith that pleased God. Pro gar tēs metathēseōs emarturēto euaresthēkenai tō theō (for before the translation he had been testified to as having pleased God — perfect infinitive: the pleasure stood as an established fact before the event).
