11.7: Pistei Nōe chrēmatistheis peri tōn mēdepō blepomenōn eulabētheis kateskeuasen kibōton eis sōtērian tou oikou autou, di' hēs katekrineken ton kosmon kai tēs kata pistin dikaiosynēs egeneto klēronōmos — "by faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family. By his faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that is in keeping with faith" (chrēmatistheis: having been divinely warned/instructed by oracle — chrēmatizō: to conduct business, to receive a divine response; same verb as 8:5 [Moses instructed]): eulabētheis: moved with godly fear, reverently careful — eulabeia: the reverent-cautious posture of one who takes seriously the divine warning. Katekrineken ton kosmon: condemned the world — not by pronouncing judgment but by his actions: his building of the ark implicitly condemned those who did not build, as his salvation made their non-response inexcusable.
11.8: Pistei kaloumenos Abraam hypēkousen exelthein eis topon hon ēmellen lambanein eis klēronomian, kai exēlthen mē epistamenos pou erchetai — "by faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go to a place he would later receive as his inheritance; and he set out without knowing where he was going" (hypēkousen: obeyed — the root of hypakoē [obedience]: literally hearing-under, submissive hearing that issues in action): the grammar is precise: kaloumenos (present passive participle: while being called) + hypēkousen (aorist: he obeyed) + exēlthen (aorist: he went out) — the obedience and departure were simultaneous with the calling, not subsequent to deliberation. Mē epistamenos pou erchetai: not knowing where he was going — the faith-obedience precedes the understanding of destination.
11.9: Pistei paroikēsen eis gēn tēs epangelias hōs allotrian, en skēnais katoikēsas meta Isaak kai Iakōb tōn synklēronōmōn tēs epangelias tēs autēs — "by faith he made his home in the promised land like a stranger in a foreign country; he lived in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob, who were heirs with him of the same promise" (paroikēsen: sojourned — the word for the resident-alien who lives in a land without citizenship; paroikos was the technical status for a foreign resident): hōs allotrian (as though it were foreign): the promised land is possessed through faith-promise, not by legal title — the very land promised is inhabited as though it belonged to others. En skēnais katoikēsas: having dwelt in tents — the tent-dwelling is a sign of the non-possessive mode of the promise: the heir lives as a guest in his own inheritance until the promise is fulfilled.
11.11: Pistei kai autē Sarra steira dynamin eis katabolēn spermatos elaben kai para kairon hēlikias, epei piston hēgēsato ton epangeilamenos — "and by faith even Sarah, who was past the age, received the ability to conceive because she considered him who had made the promise to be faithful" (autē Sarra steira: Sarah herself being barren — the very one who laughed at the promise [Gen 18:12] received what she doubted through faith): dynamin eis katabolēn spermatos: power/capacity for the laying-down of seed — katabolē: a throwing-down, founding; here the biological process of conception. Para kairon hēlikias: beyond the season of age, past the natural time — the impossibility is noted to anchor the miracle in biological fact. Epei piston hēgēsato: because she considered faithful — the faith-act is cognitive: an assessment of God's reliability.
11.12: dio kai aph' henos egennēthēsan, kai tauta nenenekrōmenou, kathōs ta astra tou ouranou tō plēthei kai hōs hē ammos hē para to cheilos tēs thalassēs hē anarithmētos — "and so from this one man, and he as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as countless as the sand on the seashore" (nenenekrōmenou: as good as dead — perfect passive participle of nekroō: having been made dead, rendered as dead; Abraham's reproductive capacity is described in the same terms as Isaac's future death-and-return [v19]): the double simile (stars of heaven + sand of the sea) draws on the covenant promises of Gen 15:5 and 22:17 — the very promises made to the one who was as good as dead. From biological death to astronomical and oceanic multitude: the contrast establishes the resurrection-logic of the covenant.
11.14: hoi gar toiauta legontes emphanizousin hoti patrida epizētousin — "people who say such things show that they are looking for a country of their own" (emphanizousin: they make manifest, they publicly show — emphanizō: to reveal, to disclose; the profession of stranger-and-sojourner status reveals the inner orientation): the public confession of being a stranger (xenos kai parepidēmos) is a declaration of loyalty: one does not confess homelessness unless one is looking for a home elsewhere. The self-designation as strangers to the present order is simultaneously a declaration of citizenship in the coming order.
11.15: kai ei men ekeinēs emnēmoneuon aph' hēs exēlthon, eichon an kairon anakampsai — "if they had been thinking of the country they had left, they would have had opportunity to return" (eichon an kairon anakampsai: they would have had an opportunity to return — second-class contrary-to-fact conditional): the argument from opportunity: those who left Mesopotamia were not prevented from returning; the roads were open, the option existed. The fact that they did not return shows the homeland they sought was not the one they left. Anakampsai: to return, to bend back — the image of turning one's path back to the point of origin.
11.16: nyn de kreittōnos oregnontai, tout' estin epouraniou; dio ouk epaischydnetai autōn ho theos theos epikaleisthai, hētoimasen gar autois polin — "instead, they were longing for a better country — a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared a city for them" (oregnontai: they long for, they stretch out for — oregomai: to reach out for, to desire with outstretched hand): ouk epaischydnetai ho theos theos epikaleisthai autōn: God is not ashamed to be called their God — the reversal of shame: the patriarchs bore the shame of homelessness; God bears the name of their God publicly and without shame. Hētoimasen polin: he has prepared a city — perfect: already done. The preparation preceded the heirs.
11.17: Pistei prosenenoken Abraam ton Isaak peirazomenos, kai ton monogenē prosepheren ho tas epangelias anadexamenos — "by faith Abraham, when God tested him, offered Isaac as a sacrifice. He who had received the promises was about to sacrifice his one and only son" (prosenenoken... prosepheren: offered... was offering — the two verb forms are significant: prosenenoken is perfect [has offered — the faith-act is completed in God's sight before the knife falls]; prosepheren is imperfect [was offering — the process was underway]): ton monogenē: the only-begotten/unique son — the same word as 11:17 and John 3:16 for Christ; the typological resonance between Isaac and Christ is built into the vocabulary. Ton tas epangelias anadexamenos: the one who had received the promises — the person who received the promise is the same one who offers the promise-bearer.
11.18: pros hon elalēthē hoti En Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma — "even though God had said to him, It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned" (Gen 21:12): the conflict is explicit: God promised seed through Isaac, then commanded the offering of Isaac. En Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma: in/through Isaac will be called to you seed — the prepositional en (in/through) indicates that Isaac is the channel of the promised lineage, not merely one of many options. The command to sacrifice Isaac is thus a direct apparent contradiction of the promise-mechanism. The faith of v17 consists precisely in proceeding despite this apparent contradiction.
11.19: logizomenos hoti kai ek nekrōn egeirein dynatos ho theos, hothen auton kai en parabolē ekomisato — "Abraham reasoned that God could even raise the dead, and so in a manner of speaking he did receive Isaac back from death" (logizomenos: reckoning, calculating, reasoning — the deliberate mental act of theological deduction): Abraham's faith is not blind but reasoned: if God can keep his promise only through Isaac, and God commands Isaac's death, then God must be able to raise the dead to fulfill the promise. En parabolē: in a parable, in a type — the recovery of Isaac is the type; the resurrection of Christ is the antitype. Isaac's survival of the intended death is a parabolic prefiguring of the resurrection.
11.20: Pistei kai peri mellontōn eulogēsen Isaak ton Iakōb kai ton Ēsau — "by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau in regard to their future" (peri mellontōn: concerning things about to come, future things): Isaac's blessing of his sons was an act of faith because the blessings concerned things entirely beyond Isaac's power to guarantee. Eulogēsen: blessed — the patriarchal blessing was not mere wish but a faith-speech that committed God to the future of the blessed. The fact that the blessing held even when it was obtained through deception (Gen 27) is evidence of divine sovereignty over the means by which the promise advances.
11.21: Pistei Iakōb apothnēskōn hekaston tōn huiōn Iōsēph eulogēsen, kai prosekunēsen epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou — "by faith Jacob, when he was dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons, and worshipped as he leaned on the top of his staff" (Gen 48:15-16; 47:31): apothnēskōn: dying — the present participle; blessing on the threshold of death is the faith-act. The detail of leaning on the staff (from Gen 47:31 LXX, where the MT has "head of the bed") is a translation difference; the LXX had rhabdon where the MT had mittah: the unpointed Hebrew allowed two readings. The posture of worship while dying is the faith-expression Hebrews highlights.
11.22: Pistei Iōsēph teleutōn peri tēs exodou tōn huiōn Israēl emnēmoneusen kai peri tōn ostōn autou eneteilato — "by faith Joseph, when he was dying, spoke of the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his bones" (Gen 50:24-25): tēs exodou: the exodus — the same word Luke uses for Jesus's departure in the Transfiguration (Luke 9:31). Joseph's faith-act is prophetic: dying in Egypt, he spoke of a future departure from Egypt and committed his bones to go with the departing people. Peri tōn ostōn autou eneteilato: gave instructions about his bones — Gen 50:25, the oath Moses honored in Exod 13:19. The bones left Egypt as a sign that Joseph's faith reached beyond his own death to the promised inheritance.
11.23: Pistei Mōysēs gennētheis ekrybē trimēnon hypo tōn paterōn autou, dioti eidon asteion to paidion kai ouk ephobēthēsan to diatagma tou basileōs — "by faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was a fine child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict" (asteion to paidion: the child was fine/beautiful — asteios: graceful, well-formed; Acts 7:20 uses asteios tō theō, beautiful to God): the faith-act is the parents', not Moses's — they saw something in the child and acted on it in defiance of royal decree. Ouk ephobēthēsan to diatagma tou basileōs: they were not afraid of the king's edict — the same structure as Moses's non-fear of the king's anger in v27.
11.25: mallon helomenos synkakouchesthai tō laō tou theou ē proskairon echein hamartias apolausin — "he chose to be mistreated along with the people of God rather than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin" (helomenos: having chosen — aorist middle participle of haireō: to take for oneself, to choose deliberately; the middle voice emphasizes the personal, self-involving character of the choice): proskairon: temporary, for a season — pros kairon: towards/for a time; the pleasures of sin are characterized by their temporality. Apolausin: enjoyment, pleasure — the Epicurean vocabulary of present pleasure; Moses's calculation reverses it: temporary pleasure vs. lasting reward.
11.27: Pistei katelipen Aigupton mē phobētheis ton thymon tou basileōs, ton gar aoroton hōs horōn ekarteresen — "by faith he left Egypt, not fearing the king's anger; he persevered because he saw him who is invisible" (ton aoroton hōs horōn: the invisible one as seeing — the paradox of faith-sight: seeing what cannot be seen physically): aoroton: the unseen, invisible one — the divine invisibility is not absence but the nature of God's being. Ekarteresen: endured, persevered — kartereō: to be strong, to hold one's ground (from kratos = strength); the endurance is strength-based, not mere resignation. Moses held his position because his sight was fixed on the one who cannot be seen by the eyes.
11.28: Pistei pepoiēken to pascha kai tēn proschusin tou haimatos, hina mē ho olothreuōn ta prōtotoka thigē autōn — "by faith he kept the Passover and the application of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch them" (pepoiēken: has kept/made — perfect active: the faith-act stands permanently): proschusin tou haimatos: the pouring/sprinkling on of blood — prochysis: a pouring forth; the application of the Passover blood to the doorposts. Ho olothreuōn: the destroyer — the destroying angel of Exod 12:23; thigē: might touch — the aorst subjunctive of thinganō: to touch, to make contact. The blood-application is the faith-act that created the boundary the destroyer could not cross.
11.29: Pistei diebēsan tēn Erythrān Thalassan hōs dia xēras gēs, hēs perian labontes hoi Aigyptioi katepothēsan — "by faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land; but when the Egyptians tried to do so, they were drowned" (diebēsan: passed through — aorist of diabainō: to cross over, to traverse): the same water that was dry land for Israel was drowning water for Egypt — the same reality, differentiated by faith. Hōs dia xēras gēs (as through dry land): the hōs (as, like) — as if it were dry land, though it was sea-bottom. Katepothēsan: were swallowed up — katapinō: to drink down, to swallow completely; the passive: the sea swallowed the Egyptians who pursued on the same basis.
11.30: Pistei ta teichē Ierichō epeson kyklōthenta epi hepta hēmeras — "by faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the army had marched around them for seven days" (epeson: fell — aorist; kyklōthenta: having been circled — aorist passive participle of kykloō: to encircle): the faith-logic: seven days of marching around walls achieves nothing militarily — the walls fell not by siege-craft but by obedience to a divine command whose rationality was not self-evident. The connection between the commanded action and the result was faith alone.
11.31: Pistei Raab hē pornē ou synap ōleto tois apeitēsasin, dexamēnē tous kataskopous met' eirēnēs — "by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she welcomed the spies in peace" (hē pornē: the prostitute — the social status is retained to heighten the faith-contrast: the Canaanite prostitute believed what Israel had heard of YHWH and acted on it): tois apeitēsasin: those who were disobedient — the same apeithia-root as the wilderness generation; the Jericho population's failure is categorized as disobedience, implying they had sufficient information to act differently. Met' eirēnēs: in peace — her welcome was not coerced but peaceful, establishing covenant-like solidarity.
11.32: kai ti eti legō; epileipsei gar me diēgoumenon ho chronos peri Gedeon, Barak, Sampson, Iephthae, Dauid te kai Samouēl kai tōn prophētōn — "and what more shall I say? Time would fail me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets" (epileipsei gar me diēgoumenon ho chronos: time will fail me as I narrate — the rhetorical device of paralipsis / praeteritio: listing what one says cannot be listed): the list order is not strictly chronological (Barak precedes Samson, but David follows Jephthah); the rhetorical move is cumulative impression, not historical sequence. Diēgoumenon: narrating — the author is aware of his own discourse, breaking the narrative frame to speak about the act of narrating.
11.34: esbesan dynamin pyros, ephygon stomata machairēs, enedunamōthēsan apo astheneias, egenethesan ischyroi en polemō, parembolas eklinan allotriōn — "who quenched the fury of the flames, and escaped the edge of the sword; whose weakness was turned to strength; and who became powerful in battle and routed foreign armies" (esbesan dynamin pyros: quenched the power of fire — the three Hebrew youths in the furnace [Dan 3]; dynamin pyros: the power of fire, not merely the fire itself): enedunamōthēsan apo astheneias: were strengthened out of weakness — apo: the weakness was the starting point from which the strengthening emerged. Parembolas eklinan allotriōn: they caused the camps of foreigners to turn, to flee — eklinō: to bend, to turn from.
11.36: heteroi de empaigmōn kai mastigōn perian labontes, eti de desmaōn kai phylakēs — "some faced jeers and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment" (empaigmōn: of mockings, derisions — from empaizō: to mock, to play-act at someone's expense; the same word for the mocking of Jesus in the passion narratives): the two tiers of suffering are noted: those who received miraculous deliverance (vv32-35) and those who suffered without physical rescue (vv36-38). Both categories are commended equally by faith. The structure prevents a prosperity-faith reading: faith does not guarantee miraculous rescue.
11.37: elethasthēsan, epristvthēsan, en phonō machairēs apethanon, periēlthon en mēlōtais, en aigeiais dermasin, hysteroumenoi, thlibomenoi, kakouchoumenoi — "they were put to death by stoning; they were sawn in two; they were killed by the sword. They went about in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, persecuted and mistreated" (epristvthēsan: they were sawn in two — traditionally applied to Isaiah [not in the OT text, but in Jewish tradition recorded in Lives of the Prophets]): en mēlōtais, en aigeiais dermasin: in sheepskins, in goat-skins — the covering of prophets, Elijah's mantle (1 Kgs 19:13); the clothing marks them as outsiders to civilization. Hysteroumenoi, thlibomenoi, kakouchoumenoi: lacking, pressed, maltreated — three progressive participles of suffering.
11.38: hōn ouk ēn axios ho kosmos, epi erēmiais planōmenoi kai oresin kai spēlaiois kai tais opais tēs gēs — "the world was not worthy of them. They wandered in deserts and mountains, and in caves and holes in the ground" (hōn ouk ēn axios ho kosmos: of whom the world was not worthy): the evaluative reversal: from the world's perspective they were marginal figures, driven out and hiding; from God's perspective they were too good for the world that rejected them. Epi erēmiais planōmenoi: wandering in desolate places — the irony is that those who sought the heavenly city were reduced to inhabiting caves and holes; the dispossession confirms the reality of their citizenship elsewhere.
11.40: tou theou peri hēmōn kreition ti problepsamenos, hina mē chōris hēmōn teleiōthōsin — "God had planned something better for us so that only together with us would they be made perfect" (problepsamenos: having foreseen and provided for beforehand — pro: before + blepō: to see; the divine foreseeing that shapes the historical arrangement): kreition ti: something better — the better thing is the completed Christ-event that the OT saints anticipated but did not receive. Hina mē chōris hēmōn teleiōthōsin: so that apart from us they would not be made perfect — the perfection is corporate: the new covenant community is the completion-context for OT faith. The cloud of witnesses cannot receive their perfection without the community for whom Christ has come.

12.1: toigaroun kai hēmeis, tosoutos echontes perikeimenon hēmin nephon martyrōn — "therefore, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses" (nephon martyrōn: cloud of witnesses — nephos: cloud, mass, great multitude; martyrōn: witnesses — genitive of apposition: a cloud that consists of witnesses): the martyres are witnesses not in the sense of spectators watching the current community's race but witnesses whose lives and deaths were themselves testimonies to faith (the sense of martyreō throughout ch11: to be approved, to testify). They witness by having witnessed — their completed lives are the evidence. Apothemenoi panta ogkon (having laid aside every weight): the athletic image of stripping for the race.
12.2: aphorōntes eis ton tēs pisteōs archēgon kai teleiōtēn Iēsoun — "looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith" (aphorōntes: looking away to, looking off toward — the apo-prefix: looking away from distractions and fixing the gaze on Jesus; archēgon: pioneer/founder — same term as 2:10; teleiōtēn: perfecter/completer — one who brings to the goal): hos anti tēs prokeimenēs autō charas hypemeinen stauron, aischynēs kataphronēsas (who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising shame): anti: instead of or in exchange for; "for the joy" or "instead of the joy" — the ambiguity is between joy that motivated endurance and joy that was set aside for endurance. Both readings are grammatically possible; the former ("motivated by") is more commonly adopted.
12.4: Oupō mechri haimatos antikatesatēsate pros tēn hamartian antagōnizomenoi — "in your struggle against sin you have not yet resisted to the point of shedding blood" (antikatesatēsate: you stood against/resisted — the athletic compound of anti [against] + kata [down] + histēmi [stand]; antagōnizomenoi: struggling against — athletic combat vocabulary): the community has suffered but not yet martyrdom. The "not yet" places their experience below the level of the exemplars of ch11 (the sawn-asunder, the tortured) and certainly below the level of Jesus's cross — calibrating the community's perception of their own suffering.
12.5: kai eklellēsthe tēs parakelseos hētis hymin hōs huiois dialegetai — "and have you forgotten the exhortation that addresses you as sons?" (eklellēsthe: have you forgotten — perfect passive of eklanthanomai: to forget completely; hōs huiois dialegetai: speaks to you as sons — the present tense of ongoing address): the Prov 3:11-12 citation is not merely past encouragement but present address — Scripture continues to speak to the community in its current situation. The forgetting is the spiritual problem; the citation is the remedy.
12.10: hoi men gar pros oligas hēmeras kata to dokoun autois epaideuon, ho de epi to sympheron eis to metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou — "for they disciplined us for a short time as it seemed best to them, but he disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness" (pros to sympheron: for the profitable/beneficial — the sympheron is the beneficial purpose vs. the arbitrary "seemed good to them" of human discipline; metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou: to partake of/receive a share of his holiness — the goal of divine discipline is holiness-participation): the divine discipline is not arbitrary suffering but purposive formation toward the community's participation in the divine character.
12.11: pasa de paideia pros men to paron ou dokei charas einai alla lypēs, hysteron de karpon eirēnikon tois di' autēs gegymnasmenos apodidōsin dikaiosynēs — "for the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it" (karpon eirēnikon: peaceful fruit — eirēnikon as an adjective modifying the fruit: the fruit has the quality of peace rather than turmoil; gegymnasmenos: having been trained/exercised — from gymnazō: to exercise naked, to train vigorously): dikaiosynēs is either subjective genitive (righteous fruit = the fruit that is righteousness) or qualitative (fruit of a righteous kind).
12.18: Ou gar proselēlythate psēlaphōmenō kai kekaumēnō pyri kai gnophō kai zophō kai thyellē — "for you have not come to what may be touched, a blazing fire and darkness and gloom and a tempest" (psēlaphōmenō: that may be touched, handled — perfect passive participle of psēlaphaō: to touch, feel, grope; the touchability of Sinai marks its earthly-material character; kekaumēnō pyri: blazing fire — perfect passive: fire that had been set burning and remains burning): the entire Sinai list is characterized as sensory, earthly, terrifying — a deliberate phenomenological contrast with the heavenly assembly of vv22-24.
12.22: alla proselēlythate Siōn orei kai polei theou zōntos, Ierousalēm epouraniō, kai myriasi angelōn, panēgyrei — "but you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering" (myriasi angelōn: myriads of angels — ten-thousands, uncountable; panēgyrei: festal gathering/assembly — the great civic or religious assembly for celebration): the distinction between panēgyrei (festal gathering of angels) and ekklēsia prōtotokōn (the assembly of the firstborn) has been debated: are these one group or two? Most take them as two separate groups within the heavenly city. Prōtotokōn (firstborn, plural): those who share the firstborn's inheritance — the community's heavenly enrollment is in the book of the firstborn-status believers.
12.24: kai diathēkēs neas mesitē Iēsou kai haimati rhantismou kreitton lalounti para ton Abel — "and to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (diathēkēs neas: new covenant — here neas [numerically new, recent] rather than the kainēs [qualitatively new] of 8:8, 13 and 9:15; the variation may be for stylistic variety or to emphasize the covenant's temporal recency): haimati rhantismou (blood of sprinkling) — the OT background of the Mosaic covenant's inaugurating blood-sprinkling (Exod 24:8; Heb 9:19-22), now applied to Christ's blood. Kreitton lalounti para ton Abel: speaking better than Abel — Abel's blood cried for justice; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness.
12.28: dio basileian asaleuton paralambanontes echōmen charin, di' hēs latreuōmen euarestōs tō theō meta eulabeias kai deous — "therefore let us be grateful for receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, and thus let us offer to God acceptable worship, with reverence and awe" (asaleuton: unshakeable, unable to be shaken — the negation of the saleusō from Hag 2:6 cited in v26): the unshakeable kingdom received through the Christological fulfillment of Haggai's shaking-promise is the basis of the community's gratitude-worship. Eulabeias kai deous: reverence and awe — the two terms of the appropriate human posture before the consuming-fire God (v29).
12.29: kai gar ho theos hēmōn pyr katanaliskōn — "for our God is a consuming fire" (pyr katanaliskōn: consuming fire — citing Deut 4:24 LXX exactly): the closing verse applies the divine consuming-fire character to the new covenant community's worship-life. The God who is consuming fire is not abandoned in the new covenant but approached with greater access — the approach requires the greater reverence that the Son's blood makes possible and necessary.
12.3: analogisasthe gar ton toiautēn hypomenēkota hypo tōn hamartoōlōn eis heauton antilogian — "consider him who endured such opposition from sinners against himself, so that you will not grow weary and lose heart" (analogisasthe: reckon up, consider carefully — aorist imperative of analogizomai: to reason through, to calculate analogically; the ana-prefix suggests careful re-counting): antilogian: contradiction, opposition, hostile speech — anti + logia: speaking-against; the word covers verbal hostility, contradiction, and the formal legal challenge. Kamēte tais psychais hymōn eklyomenoi (lest you become weary, fainting in your souls): eklyō: to release the tension, to go slack — the opposite of the taut focus on Jesus that the verse urges.
12.6: hon gar agapāi Kyrios paideuei, mastigoi de panta huion hon paradechetai — "because the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and he chastens everyone he accepts as his son" (Prov 3:12 LXX): mastigoi: scourges, flogs — a stronger word than paideuei (disciplines); the Proverbs text escalates from discipline to scourging to convey the full force of corrective love. Hon agapāi... hon paradechetai (the one he loves... the one he receives/accepts): the divine discipline is restricted to those within the relationship — it is a mark of belonging, not rejection. The parallel structure of the two verbs (discipline/love; scourge/accept) makes the same point twice with different vocabulary.
12.7: eis paideian hypomenete; hōs huiois hymin prospheretai ho theos; tis gar huios hon ou paideuei patēr — "endure hardship as discipline; God is treating you as his children. For what children are not disciplined by their father?" (eis paideian hypomenete: endure with a view to/for discipline — the eis gives the purpose: endurance is not mere stoicism but intentional orientation toward the formative goal): hōs huiois hymin prospheretai ho theos (God deals with you as with sons — prospheretai: present tense ongoing dealing): the suffering is not evidence of abandonment but evidence of the Father's engaged parental attention. The rhetorical question (tis gar huios: for what son?) assumes universal affirmative: all sons receive discipline.
12.8: ei de chōris este paideias, hēs metochoi gegonasi pantes, ara nothoi kai ouch huioi este — "if you are not disciplined — and everyone undergoes discipline — then you are not legitimate children but illegitimate" (nothoi: illegitimate, bastard-born — a legal term for a child born outside of recognized marriage; ouch huioi: not sons [in the legal sense of recognized, inheriting sons]): the social reality behind the word: a nothos child was not educated by the father and did not share in the father's estate. The absence of discipline signals the absence of the paternal relationship — not divine indifference but non-belonging.
12.9: eita tous men tēs sarkos hēmōn pateras eichomen paideutos kai enetrepometha; ou poly mallon hypotakēsometha tō patri tōn pneumatōn kai zēsomen — "moreover, we have all had human fathers who disciplined us and we respected them for it. How much more should we submit to the Father of spirits and live" (pateras tēs sarkos: fathers of the flesh — the bodily, genetic fathers in contrast to ton patera tōn pneumatōn [the Father of spirits]): enetrepometha: we respected, showed deference — entrepō: to turn toward with respect; the a minori ad maius argument: if earthly-biological fathers earned submission through imperfect discipline, the Father whose essence is Spirit and whose discipline is perfect deserves infinitely more. Kai zēsomen (and we will live): submission to the Father of spirits produces life — the submission is not mere social compliance but life-giving covenantal response.
12.12: dio tas paralelymenas cheiras kai ta paralyelymena gonata anorthōsate — "therefore, strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees" (Isa 35:3 echoed): paralelymenas cheiras: hands that have been made slack/drooping — paralyelymenas: perfect passive of paralyō: to release the side tension, to go limp; the cognate of our word "paralysis": paralelymenas gonata (paralyzed/drooping knees): the two body-parts that fail under sustained exertion — the hands that lose their grip and the knees that buckle. The command is paradoxical: the drooping hands are told to strengthen themselves. The imperatives assume that the community has agency even in exhaustion — the call to action is itself a form of strengthening.
12.13: kai trochas orthas poieite tois posin hymōn, hina mē to chōlon ektrapē, iathē de mallon — "make level paths for your feet, so that the lame may not be disabled, but rather healed" (Prov 4:26 echoed; cf. Isa 35:3): trochas orthas poieite: make straight/level tracks — trochias: wheel-tracks, the ruts made by wheels; paths made straight enough for the injured to walk without stumbling. To chōlon: the lame one, the limping member — the community contains members who are weak and close to failing; the stronger members' straight-walking creates the conditions for the weaker to recover rather than collapse. Ektrapē... iathē: be turned aside... be healed — the choice of paths determines the outcome for the vulnerable.
12.14: Eirēnēn diōkete meta pantōn kai ton hagiasmon, hou chōris oudeis opsetai ton Kyrion — "make every effort to live in peace with everyone and to be holy; without holiness no one will see the Lord" (diōkete: pursue, chase — the verb of active hunting/pursuit; peace is not a passive condition but an active quest): hagiasmon: sanctification, consecration — the process and state of being set apart; not merely moral improvement but the condition of being appropriately related to the holy God. Hou chōris oudeis opsetai ton Kyrion: without which no one will see the Lord — the eschatological vision of God (the beatific vision) is conditioned on holiness. The warning echoes Ps 24:3-4 (who may stand in the holy place? Those with clean hands and pure hearts).
12.15: episkopountes mē tis husterōn apo tēs charitos tou theou, mē tis riza pikrias anō phyousa enochla kai di' autēs mianthōsin hoi polloi — "see to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God and that no bitter root grows up to cause trouble and defile many" (episkopountes: overseeing, looking over carefully — the community exercise of pastoral mutual-care; husterōn apo tēs charitos: falling short of, falling behind from the grace of God — hysterein apo: to be separated from by falling behind): riza pikrias: root of bitterness (Deut 29:17 LXX): the agricultural metaphor of a poisonous root spreading underground and contaminating what grows from it; bitterness is insidious because it operates beneath the surface and spreads before it is visible. Mianthōsin hoi polloi: the many be defiled — the singular root affects the corporate community.
12.16: mē tis pornos ē bebēlos hōs Ēsau, hos anti brōseōs mias apedeto ta prōtotokia heautou — "see that no one is sexually immoral, or is godless like Esau, who for a single meal sold his inheritance rights as the oldest son" (bebēlos: profane, godless — one who crosses into the sacred realm with improper foot, who treats what is set-apart as common; brōseōs mias: a single eating/meal — brōsis: the eating-act, food): Esau is paired with pornos (sexually immoral) not because the two sins are equated but because both represent the exchange of the permanent for the immediate-sensory. Prōtotokia: birthright, firstborn privileges — the entire future inheritance-claim exchanged for a bowl of stew. The sacrilege (bebēlos) is the measure by which Hebrews frames Esau: not sexual but covenantal failure.
12.17: iste gar hoti kai metepeita thelōn klēronomēsai tēn eulogian apedokimasthē, metanoias gar topon ouch heuren, kaitoi meta dakryon ekzētēsas autēn — "for you know that afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected. Even though he sought the blessing with tears, he could not change what he had done" (apedokimasthē: was rejected — aorist passive of apodokimazō: to reject after testing, to pronounce unfit; cf. the rejected stone of Ps 118:22): metanoias topon ouch heuren: found no place of repentance — the phrase is ambiguous: did he find no place to repent, or did he find no place to change the outcome? The Greek metanoia can mean either. In context, the topon (place) likely means the opportunity to reverse the outcome — the irreversibility of the birthright transaction is the pastoral warning.
12.19: kai salpiggos ēchō kai phōnē rhēmatōn, hēs hoi akousantes parētēsanto mē prostethēnai autois logon — "to the blast of a trumpet and to such a voice speaking words that those who heard it begged that no further word be spoken to them" (salpingos ēchō: the sound of a trumpet — Sinai's characteristic soundscape [Exod 19:16, 19]): parētēsanto: begged off, asked to be excused — paraiteomai: to ask to be removed from something, to excuse oneself; the same verb as 12:25 (do not refuse him who speaks). The people at Sinai could not bear the weight of direct divine address — their refusal is not disobedience but the recognition of creaturely incapacity before unmediated divine speech.
12.20: ouk epheron gar to diastelloumenon, Kan thērion thigē tou orous, lithobolēthēsetai — "because they could not bear what was commanded: If even an animal touches the mountain, it must be stoned" (Exod 19:12-13): ouk epheron: they could not bear/carry — the weight of the divine command was unbearable; to diastelloumenon: what was commanded/ordered — the present passive participle of diasstellō: to command definitively, to give decisive orders. Kan thērion thigē: even if a beast touches — the most minimal, innocent contact with the mountain incurred capital punishment for the animal; the holiness-gradient at Sinai was absolute and death-dealing.
12.21: kai houtōs phoberotaton ēn to phantazomenon, Mōysēs eipen, Ekphobos eimi kai entromos — "the sight was so terrifying that Moses said, I am trembling with fear" (phoberotaton ēn to phantazomenon: the spectacle was absolutely terrifying — phoberōtatos is the superlative of phoberos: the most terrifying possible; phantazomenon: the appearing, the thing made visible — the theophanic spectacle): Ekphobos eimi kai entromos: I am struck with terror and trembling — the two compounds (ek-phobos: terror-struck; en-tromos: shaking-inwardly) together convey total physical and psychological overwhelm. The attribution to Moses of words from the Sinai theophany combines Deut 9:19 (Moses's fear at the golden-calf incident) with the Sinai context — a free composite citation.
12.23: ekklēsia prōtotokōn apographomenōn en ouranois, kai kritē theō pantōn, kai pneumasin dikaiōn teteleiōmenōn — "to the assembly of the firstborn, whose names are written in heaven. You have come to God, the Judge of all, to the spirits of the righteous made perfect" (prōtotokōn apographomenōn: of firstborn ones enrolled — apographō: to register, to enroll in a public census or citizenship list; the heavenly enrollment is the eschatological citizenship registration): ekklēsia prōtotokōn: the assembly of the firstborn — the plural prōtotokōn extends the firstborn status (held uniquely by the Son) to all who share in Christ's inheritance. Pneumasin dikaiōn teteleiōmenōn: spirits of the righteous who have been made perfect — the OT saints of ch11 now perfected in Christ (cf. 11:40); they are pneumata (spirits) because their resurrection bodies are still awaited.
12.25: Blepete mē paraitēsēsthe ton lalounta; ei gar ekeinoi ouk exephygon epi gēs paraitēsamenoi ton chrēmatizonta, poly mallon hēmeis hoi ton ap' ouranōn apostraphentes — "see to it that you do not refuse him who speaks. If they did not escape when they refused him who warned them on earth, how much less will we, if we turn away from him who warns us from heaven" (paraitēsēsthe: refuse, beg off — aorist subjunctive; the same word used in v19 for Israel's refusal at Sinai): the structure is the familiar a minori ad maius argument: if the earthly theophany's rejection was fatal, the heavenly one's rejection is infinitely more so. Ton chrēmatizonta: the one who divinely warned — chrēmatizō: to receive a divine oracle, to be divinely instructed (cf. 8:5 of Moses). Ap' ouranōn apostraphentes: those turning away from heaven — the turn-away is directional, leaving the heavenly voice behind.
12.26: hou hē phōnē tēn gēn esaleusen tote, nyn de epēggeltai legōn, Eti hapax egō seiō ou monon tēn gēn alla kai ton ouranon — "at that time his voice shook the earth, but now he has promised: Once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens" (Hag 2:6 LXX): esaleusen: shook — aorist; the Sinai earth-shaking is past and completed. Eti hapax: yet once more — the eschatological shaking is introduced by eti hapax: not a repeated event in the Sinai series but a final definitive shaking that goes beyond the earth to the heavens. Ou monon... alla kai (not only... but also): the scope of the coming shaking exceeds all previous ones — both created realms are shaken.
12.27: to de Eti hapax dēloi tēn tōn saleuomenōn metastasin hōs pepoiēmenōn, hina meinē ta mē saleuomena — "the words 'once more' indicate the removing of what can be shaken — that is, created things — so that what cannot be shaken may remain" (metastasin: removal, translation, transformation — from methistēmi: to remove, transfer, displace; a NT word for both physical removal and metaphorical transformation): tōn saleuomenōn vs. ta mē saleuomena: the shakeable vs. the unshakeable — the contrast structures the eschatological hermeneutic. Hōs pepoiēmenōn (as having been made — perfect passive participle): that which was made/created is by nature shakeable because it was made at a point in time from nothing. What was not made (the kingdom of v28) cannot be shaken because it does not belong to the created order.

13.2: tēs philoxenias mē epilanthanese, dia tautēs gar elathon tines xenisontes angelous — "do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels without knowing it" (philoxenias: love-of-stranger, hospitality — philos + xenos: affection toward the foreigner; elathon: escaped notice, were unaware — aorist of lanthanō: to escape notice): the construction elathon... xenisontes (escaped notice while showing hospitality to) is the idiom for doing something without realizing it. The Genesis precedent (Abraham and Lot) establishes that ordinary hospitality can be divine encounter.
13.7: mnēmoneuete tōn hēgoumenōn hymōn, hoitines elalēsan hymin ton logon tou theou, hōn anatheoroyntes tēn ekbasin tēs anastrophēs, mimeisthe tēn pistin — "remember your leaders, those who spoke to you the word of God. Consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith" (hēgoumenōn: leaders, those who lead — the present active participle as a title: those who exercise leadership): the specific "outcome" (ekbasis: exit, outcome, result — cf. 1 Cor 10:13) of the leaders' lives suggests these are leaders who have died — possibly martyrs whose death-pattern the community is to contemplate and emulate. Mimeisthe tēn pistin: imitate their faith — the faith-quality is the object of imitation, not specific actions.
13.8: Iēsous Christos echthes kai sēmeron ho autos kai eis tous aiōnas — "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" (echthes: yesterday; sēmeron: today; eis tous aiōnas: unto the ages, forever): the temporal statement covers past-present-future in one compact predication. In context (after the call to remember past leaders and before the warning against strange teachings), the unchangeableness of Christ grounds the community's resistance to doctrinal novelty — what was taught by the past leaders under the eternal Christ remains valid under the same eternal Christ. The verse is not a general statement of divine immutability but a contextual anchor against theological drift.
13.10: echomen thysiastērion ex hou phagein ouk echousin exousian hoi tē skēnē latreuontes — "we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat" (thysiastērion: altar — the place of sacrifice; exousian phagein: right/authority to eat): the altar-eating privilege was a genuine feature of the Levitical system: the priests ate portions of certain sacrifices. The community's altar is the cross; the eating is participation in the benefits of Christ's sacrifice (cf. 1 Cor 10:18 on the relationship between eating and altar-participation). Those who maintain the tent-system forfeit the right to eat from the Christological altar.
13.12: dio kai Iēsous, hina hagiasē dia tou idiou haimatos ton laon, exō tēs pylēs epathen — "so Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood" (exō tēs pylēs: outside the gate — the Jerusalem city gate; the crucifixion site outside the city walls is the typological fulfillment of the atonement-animals burned outside the camp): hina hagiasē... ton laon (that he might sanctify the people): the purpose of the outside-the-gate suffering is the sanctification of the people — the same purpose as the burning of the sin-offering outside the camp. The spatial dislocation of the sacrifice is the mechanism of the community's purification.
13.15: di' autou oun anapherōmen thysian aineseōs dia pantos tō theō, tout' estin karpon cheiloōn homologountōn tō onomati autou — "through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name" (anapherōmen thysian aineseōs: let us offer up a sacrifice of praise — anapherō: to carry up, to bring to the altar; the same verb used for the Aaronic offerings [5:1] and for Christ bearing sins [9:28]): karpon cheiloōn homologountōn (fruit of lips that confess/acknowledge): Hos 14:2 LXX uses karpon cheiloōn (fruit of lips) as the replacement for animal sacrifice after judgment — Hebrews inherits this prophetic substitution and grounds it in Christ's completed sacrifice.
13.20: Ho de theos tēs eirēnēs, ho anagagōn ek nekrōn ton poimena tōn probatōn ton megan en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou, ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun — "now may the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant" (anagagōn ek nekrōn: having brought up from the dead — anagō: to bring up/lead up; the unusual verb choice [not the standard egeirō = raise] recalls the Shepherd-from-the-sea imagery of Isa 63:11; the bringing-up from death echoes the bringing-up from Egypt): en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou: by/in the blood of the eternal covenant — the resurrection is grounded in the eternal covenant sealed by blood; the blood precedes and grounds the resurrection. Ton poimena ton megan: the great shepherd — the superlative characterization of the risen Christ as shepherd fulfills the OT shepherd-messiah hope (Ezek 34:23-24; 37:24).
13.1: Hē philadelphia menetō — "let brotherly love continue" (philadelphia: love of brothers — philos [affection/friendship-love] + adelphos [brother]; the compound covers the warm, familial bond of the community): menetō: let it remain, let it continue — present imperative of menō: to stay, to abide; the call is not to establish what is new but to maintain what already exists. The word choice philadelphia rather than agapē is notable — the sibling-affection word is appropriate for a community whose members are the adelphoi (brothers and sisters) of the Son who is not ashamed to call them such (2:11-12).
13.3: mimnēskesthe tōn desmion hōs syndedemenoi, tōn kakouchoumenōn hōs kai autoi ontes en sōmati — "remember those who are in prison as if you were their fellow prisoners, and those who are mistreated as if you yourselves were suffering" (hōs syndedemenoi: as if bound-together-with — syndesmoi: co-prisoners; the syn-prefix expresses the imaginative solidarity): hōs kai autoi ontes en sōmati: as yourselves also being in the body — en sōmati: the bodily existence that makes suffering real and shareable; the reminder that the community shares the same physical vulnerability. The identification is not merely empathetic but bodily — the same flesh is the site of both comfort and suffering.
13.4: Timios ho gamos en pasin kai hē koitē amiantos; pornous gar kai moichous krinei ho theos — "let marriage be held in honor by all, and let the marriage bed be kept pure, for God will judge the sexually immoral and the adulterous" (timios: honored, precious — the same word for "precious" in 1 Pet 1:19 of Christ's blood): koitē amiantos: undefiled bed — koitē: the marriage bed as a metonymy for the sexual relationship; amiantos: unstained, same word as 7:26 for Christ as unstained. Pornous kai moichous krinei ho theos: God judges the sexually immoral and the adulterous — present tense: the divine judgment is ongoing and certain; pornos (one who practices sexual immorality) and moichos (adulterer, one who violates the marriage covenant) cover both pre-marital and extra-marital violations.
13.5: Aphilēargyros ho tropos; arkoumenoi tois parousin; autos gar eirēken, Ou mē se anō oud' ou mē se egkataleipō — "keep your life free from love of money, and be content with what you have, for he has said: Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you" (aphilēargyros ho tropos: let your manner of life be free from love of silver/money — a-privative + philos + argyros: the compound adjective; tropos: manner of life, disposition): Ou mē se anō oud' ou mē se egkataleipō: the double negative ou mē used twice, the strongest possible Greek negation, translating a Hebrew emphatic promise (Josh 1:5; Deut 31:6, 8): the divine promise of non-abandonment is given as the logical ground for the contentment-command — one who holds the divine promise of presence has no rational basis for anxious acquisitiveness.
13.6: hōste tharroūntas hēmas legein, Kyrios emoi boēthos, [kai] ou phobēthēsomai; ti poiēsei moi anthrōpos — "so we say with confidence: The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can anyone do to me?" (Ps 118:6 LXX): tharroūntas: having confidence, being bold — present active participle of tharreō: to be bold, to take courage; the confidence produces the declaration. Boēthos: helper, one who runs to the cry (cf. 2:18: boēthēsai) — the divine title echoes the high-priestly help of the earlier chapters. Ti poiēsei moi anthrōpos: what shall a human being do to me? — anthrōpos here is the mortal human in contrast to the divine Helper; the rhetorical question assumes the answer: nothing that matters ultimately.
13.9: didachais poikilais kai xenais mē parapheresthe; kalon gar chariti bebaiouesthai tēn kardian, ou brōmasin, en hois ouk ōphelēthēsan hoi peripatountes — "do not be carried away by all kinds of strange teachings. It is good for the heart to be strengthened by grace, not by food regulations, which are of no benefit to those who observe them" (didachais poikilais kai xenais: various and foreign teachings — poikilos: many-colored, varied, diverse; xenos: foreign, strange, belonging to another tradition; the combination signals novelty and foreignness): parapheresthe: do not be carried away/swept along — the nautical image recurs (cf. 2:1, pararrōmen); chariti: by grace — the contrast between charis and brōmata (foods) is the theological point: what stabilizes the heart is grace (the new covenant gift), not the observance of dietary regulations whose ritual significance has been superseded.
13.11: hōn gar eisipheretai zōōn to haima peri hamartias eis ta hagia dia tou archiereōs, toutōn ta sōmata katakaietai exō tēs parembolas — "for the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the Most Holy Place by the high priest as a sin offering are burned outside the camp" (Lev 16:27): exō tēs parembolas: outside the camp — the Levitical sin-offering whose blood entered the sanctuary had its carcass burned outside the camp (not consumed); the blood went in, the body went out. Parembola: camp — the word for the Israelite wilderness camp; Hebrews uses it deliberately to frame the typology in Mosaic terms before applying it to Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem's gate (v12).
13.13: toinun exerchōmetha pros auton exō tēs parembolas, ton oneidismon autou pherontes — "let us, then, go to him outside the camp, bearing the disgrace he bore" (toinun exerchōmetha: therefore let us go out — the inferential particle toinun introduces the application drawn from the typology; exerchōmetha: hortatory subjunctive: the author includes himself in the call): exō tēs parembolas (outside the camp): the spatial movement required of the community — going out from the security of the religious establishment that crucified Christ and joining him in the social margins. Ton oneidismon autou pherontes: bearing his disgrace/reproach — the same oneidismos that Moses bore (11:26); the reproach is not incidental to the journey but its content.
13.14: ou gar echomen hōde menousan polin, alla tēn mellousan epizētoumen — "for here we do not have an enduring city, but we are looking for the city that is to come" (menousan polin: an abiding/remaining city — menousa: present participle of menō: the city that remains, the permanent city): the city-language binds together the entire letter's climax: the patriarchs looked for a city (11:10, 16), Mount Zion is the heavenly Jerusalem (12:22), and the community has no permanent earthly city. Tēn mellousan: the coming one — the same mellō root as the coming age, the coming good things; the community's citizenship is in the city that is still arriving.
13.16: tēs de eupoiias kai koinōnias mē epilanthanethe; toiautais gar thysiais euaresteitai ho theos — "and do not forget to do good and to share with others, for with such sacrifices God is pleased" (eupoiia: doing good, beneficence — eu + poieō: the compound for well-doing; a NT hapax): koinōnias: sharing, fellowship — the economic and social solidarity of the community. Toiautais thysiais: with such sacrifices — the vocabulary of sacrifice is applied to concrete acts of generosity; the sacrificial system has not simply ended but been transformed: the offerings God receives now are praise (v15) and beneficence (v16). The two new-covenant sacrifices match the old covenant's two dimensions: the upward offering to God (praise) and the horizontal sharing with others.
13.17: Peithesthe tois hēgoumenois hymōn kai hypeikete; autoi gar agrypnousin hyper tōn psychōn hymōn hōs logon apodōsontes — "have confidence in your leaders and submit to their authority, because they keep watch over you as those who must give an account" (agrypnousin: they keep watch, lie awake for — present tense: the ongoing watchfulness; agrypneō: to be sleepless, to lie awake watching; the image of a shepherd who cannot sleep while the flock is in danger): hōs logon apodōsontes: as those about to give an account — apodidōmi: to give back, to render; the account is rendered to God, not to the community. The leaders' accountability to God is both the ground of their authority and the limit of it. Hina meta charas touto poiōsin kai mē stenazontes: so that they do this with joy and not with groaning.
13.18: Proseuchesthe peri hēmōn; peithometha gar hoti kalēn syneidēsin echomen — "pray for us. We are sure that we have a clear conscience and desire to live honorably in every way" (kalēn syneidēsin: a beautiful/good conscience — kalos: good in the qualitative sense of beauty and excellence; the syneidēsis [conscience] that the sprinkled blood of Christ has cleansed [9:14] is now described as kalē): en pasin kalōs thelontes anastraphēnai: desiring to conduct ourselves well in all things — anastraphēnai: to conduct oneself, to live; the conscience claim is accompanied by the desire for corresponding conduct. The prayer-request grounds the author's pastoral authority in personal integrity.
13.19: perissoteros de parakalō touto poiēsai, hina tachion apokatastathō hymin — "I particularly urge you to pray so that I may be restored to you soon" (perissoteros parakalō: I urge more abundantly/especially — the superlative adverb intensifying the urgency): apokatastathō: be restored — aorist passive subjunctive of apokathistēmi: to restore fully to the original position; the same verb for eschatological restoration. Tachion: more quickly, sooner — comparative of tachus: the author's desire is for an expedited reunion. The personal note grounds the theological treatise in an actual relationship between author and community.
13.21: katartisai hymas en panti agathō eis to poiēsai to thelēma autou, poiōn en hēmin to euareston enōpion autou dia Iēsou Christou — "equip you with everything good for doing his will, and may he work in us what is pleasing to him, through Jesus Christ" (katartisai: equip, restore, fit out — the same verb as 10:5 [body you prepared/fitted] and Eph 4:12 [equipping of the saints]): to euareston: what is pleasing, the pleasing thing — the verbal noun captures the quality of divine approval; poiōn en hēmin: working in us — God is the agent of both the equipping and the performing; the prayer acknowledges that the doing of God's will is itself a divine work within the community. Dia Iēsou Christou: through Jesus Christ — the mediatorial channel of all divine working.
13.22: Parakalō de hymas, adelphoi, anechesthe tou logou tēs paraklēseōs, kai gar dia bracheōn epestila hymin — "brothers and sisters, I urge you to bear with my word of exhortation, for in fact I have written to you quite briefly" (logou tēs paraklēseōs: word of exhortation/encouragement — paraklēsis: the calling-alongside to encourage, strengthen, or exhort; a self-identification of the letter's genre): dia bracheōn epestila: I wrote through brief/short means — dia bracheōn: briefly, concisely; the author is aware that what the community might find difficult to receive has been presented as briefly as possible. The letter is long by NT standards; "briefly" is relative to the depth of the material covered.
13.23: ginōskete ton adelphon Timotheon apolelumenon, meth' hou, ean tachion erchētai, opsōmai hymas — "I want you to know that our brother Timothy has been released. If he arrives soon, I will come with him to see you" (apolelumenon: having been released — perfect passive participle of apoluō: to release, set free; the same word for releasing from prison or for the Levitical dismissal): the mention of Timothy (ton adelphon Timotheon: the brother Timothy) and his release from some form of custody provides the one unambiguous personal identification in the letter. The conditional ean tachion erchētai (if he arrives soon enough) ties the author's intended visit to Timothy's freedom of travel.
13.24: Aspasasthe pantas tous hēgoumenous hymōn kai pantas tous hagious; aspazontai hymas hoi apo tēs Italias — "greet all your leaders and all the Lord's people. Those from Italy send you their greetings" (Aspasasthe: greet — the standard epistolary closing formula): pantas... pantas (all... all): the double pantas emphasizes comprehensiveness — no leader and no holy person is excluded from the greeting. Hoi apo tēs Italias: those from Italy — a phrase that can mean either those currently in Italy (writing to the recipients) or Italian expatriates who are with the author and send greetings back home; the latter reading supports the hypothesis of Roman recipients.
13.25: Hē charis meta pantōn hymōn — "grace be with you all" (Hē charis: the grace — with the article: a specific grace, the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ): the minimal epistolary benediction. Meta pantōn hymōn: with all of you — the pantōn echoes the universal scope of the greeting in v24; the closing grace encompasses the entire community. Charis as the closing word gathers the theological heart of the letter: the grace mediated by the eternal high priest who entered once for all into the heavenly sanctuary is the final word to the community that is called to draw near with confidence.
